proposals.title: INVERT: Inducing Natural-language Verifiable Task Rules from Examples
proposals.motivation: Current instruction-following models require explicit, high-quality human instructions, which are costly to create and can be ambiguous. This limits the adaptability of models to new tasks defined only by examples, a common scenario in few-shot learning and real-world data. Inverting the paradigm to generate instructions from examples could enable automated task documentation, improve model interpretability, and facilitate instruction discovery from observed behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model can be trained to generate accurate, generalizable, and human-verifiable task instructions when conditioned solely on a few input-output examples, and this ability will improve the model's own performance on related inference tasks.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a large language model (e.g., OLMo) using a novel training objective. The model will be trained on a curated dataset of (input-output examples, instruction) pairs, where the instruction is treated as the target sequence. We will leverage the Infini-gram framework for efficient, large-scale retrieval of relevant in-context examples during training to improve generalization. The generated instructions will be evaluated for correctness by using them to guide a separate, frozen model's performance on held-out examples, creating a closed-loop, human-in-the-loop verification system inspired by fine-grained hallucination detection techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new task and benchmark for instruction induction. It will provide a method for automatically documenting and clarifying the implicit task underlying a set of demonstrations, advancing the field of model interpretability and meta-reasoning. The resulting models could serve as tools for human analysts to understand model behavior or rapidly prototype instructions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on following or editing instructions, we propose to *generate* the foundational instruction itself from demonstrations, a core inversion of the standard control paradigm. This moves beyond simple task identification to the synthesis of explicit, compositional natural language rules.

proposals.title: Instructing Compositional Generalization: A Fine-Grained Benchmark and Analysis of Task Composition via Language
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) excel at following instructions for known tasks but struggle with compositional generalization—systematically combining known skills to solve novel, unseen tasks. This is a critical limitation for real-world deployment, where users need models to adapt to complex, evolving requirements. A systematic study of how instruction-following ability breaks down under novel task compositions is needed to diagnose and improve model robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current LLMs, despite strong in-context learning, will show significant performance degradation on instruction-defined tasks that are novel compositions of sub-tasks they can perform individually, and that this failure can be traced to specific inference-time bottlenecks in parsing and executing compositional structure.
proposals.method: We will create a fine-grained benchmark of tasks defined as novel compositions of atomic operations (e.g., 'summarize then translate' vs. 'translate then summarize'). Building on Hajishirzi's work on fine-grained hallucination detection and Infini-gram's scalable n-gram analysis, we will design controlled experiments using models like OLMo. We will analyze failure modes by tracing model inferences against the Infini-gram suffix tree to identify where compositional instructions diverge from seen n-gram patterns, and introduce targeted fine-tuning strategies that explicitly train on compositional task descriptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new diagnostic benchmark for compositional generalization via instruction following, 2) a detailed analysis linking generalization failures to statistical deviations from training data, and 3) preliminary fine-tuning methods that improve robustness to novel task compositions, advancing towards more reliable and adaptable language models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies compositional generalization in synthetic semantic parsing, our approach uniquely focuses on instruction-following in modern LLMs, using scalable n-gram analysis (Infini-gram) for fine-grained failure diagnosis, bridging the gap between statistical language modeling and structured task execution.

proposals.title: MegaBench: A Billion-Scale Synthetic Task Benchmark for Probing and Improving Generalization
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task benchmarks, while valuable, are limited to hundreds or thousands of tasks, potentially failing to expose systematic generalization failures in large language models (LLMs). As models scale, we need evaluation at a commensurate scale to rigorously test the breadth and limits of their capabilities, particularly for fine-grained hallucination detection and editing. A massive, diverse task corpus can serve as a high-resolution diagnostic tool and a training substrate for more robust models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that generating a benchmark with orders of magnitude more synthetic tasks will reveal latent, systematic failure modes in LLMs not captured by existing benchmarks, and that training or fine-tuning models on this expanded distribution will lead to improved generalization and robustness on real-world tasks.
proposals.method: We will leverage a powerful base LLM (e.g., OLMo 3) and the Infini-gram index to generate and validate tasks at scale. First, we will use Infini-gram to mine trillion-token corpora for diverse patterns, relations, and knowledge snippets as task seeds. A controlled generation pipeline will then synthesize tasks (e.g., QA, entailment, editing) by applying structured transformations and constraints to these seeds. Finally, we will employ self-consistency checks, Infini-gram-based factual verification, and human-in-the-loop validation on a subset to ensure task quality and diversity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) MegaBench, the first publicly available benchmark with millions to billions of machine-generated tasks for evaluating generalization; 2) A systematic analysis of how LLM performance and failure modes scale with the number and diversity of tasks; 3) Evidence on whether training on such a synthetic corpus reduces fine-grained hallucinations and improves performance on held-out real benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the unprecedented scale of task creation, enabled by the synergistic use of a trillion-token n-gram index (Infini-gram) for grounded seed discovery and a state-of-the-art LLM for constrained generation, moving beyond manually curated or small-scale synthetic benchmarks.

proposals.title: Inverse Prompt Scaling: Maximizing Task Performance with Minimal Instructions and Maximal In-Context Examples
proposals.motivation: Current instruction tuning and prompting paradigms rely heavily on detailed, often verbose, natural language instructions to specify tasks, which is costly to generate and may not align with how models best learn from context. Recent work on in-context learning and scaling laws suggests that the number and quality of examples are paramount, but the precise trade-off between instruction explicitness and example volume remains unexplored. This study investigates whether we can achieve superior or comparable performance by radically reducing instruction length while proportionally increasing the number of in-context demonstrations, potentially leading to more data-efficient and robust task specification.
proposals.hypothesis: For a fixed context window budget, language models achieve higher and more reliable performance on a diverse set of tasks when provided with minimal, even templated, instructions but a maximized number of in-context examples, compared to standard verbose instruction-following formats. This 'inverse scaling' effect is most pronounced for tasks requiring procedural or compositional reasoning.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled experiment using the OLMo model family and other open-source LLMs. We will define a spectrum of instruction formats, from verbose (e.g., Alpaca-style) to minimal (e.g., a single task delimiter like "Q:" and "A:"). For each format, we will systematically vary the number of in-context examples within a fixed token budget, evaluating performance on benchmarks from BIG-Bench Hard, MMLU, and specialized tasks requiring factual grounding. We will employ the Infini-gram framework for efficient, large-scale n-gram analysis of model outputs to quantify patterns of hallucination and reasoning consistency, as measured by metrics from fine-grained hallucination detection work. Statistical analysis will identify optimal trade-off curves.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of the instruction-example trade-off, yielding practical guidelines for efficient prompt engineering and instruction dataset construction. It will contribute a novel perspective on scaling laws, shifting focus from model and data scale to the optimal allocation of context space. The findings could inform the design of next-generation training and inference protocols that prioritize example density over instruction verbosity.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws typically study model or dataset size, this proposal inverts the problem to study the scaling of two components within a fixed context window. It directly challenges the prevailing assumption that high-quality, detailed instructions are always necessary for optimal in-context learning, proposing a counter-intuitive, data-driven alternative.

proposals.title: The Supervision Spectrum: A Unified Framework for Granular Control from Full Supervision to Zero-Shot Instruction Following
proposals.motivation: Current instruction tuning for large language models operates in a binary regime: models are either trained on massive, task-specific datasets or prompted in a zero-shot manner, with little control over the granularity of supervision. This limits our ability to understand how supervision density influences model generalization, robustness, and potential for hallucination. A principled continuum of supervision is needed to systematically study this relationship and enable more efficient, controllable model adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing a fine-grained, interpolatable 'supervision spectrum'—from thousands of examples per task down to zero—we can develop models and diagnostic tools that predictably trade off data efficiency for task performance and reveal the minimal supervision needed for reliable instruction following.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Infini-gram framework to create a massive, trillion-token corpus of (instruction, response) pairs, enabling precise measurement of n-gram-based 'supervision proximity' for any new instruction. We will then train a model like OLMo 3 using a novel 'spectral tuning' objective that interpolates between a standard supervised loss and a retrieval-augmented, distributionally-grounded loss based on Infini-gram statistics. This creates a continuous control parameter for supervision density. Fine-grained hallucination detection methods will be used to evaluate output fidelity at each point on the spectrum.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel training framework for models with a tunable supervision parameter, 2) a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating models across the supervision spectrum, and 3) empirical insights into the non-linear relationships between example count, generalization, and hallucination propensity in instruction following.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores few-shot learning, our approach is the first to formulate supervision as a continuous, model-internal spectrum and to use unbounded n-gram statistics as a grounding mechanism to facilitate the transition to zero-shot generalization, moving beyond simple example counting.

proposals.title: Syntax-Aware and Entity-Grounded Embeddings for Improved Factuality and Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) rely on dense, opaque token embeddings that conflate syntactic, semantic, and factual information, potentially contributing to hallucination and inefficiency in factual recall. Pre-2026 work, such as Hajishirzi's research on fine-grained hallucination detection and the scaling of n-gram models (Infini-gram), highlights the need for more interpretable and structured internal representations to improve model truthfulness and inference-time precision.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly incorporating syntactic dependency roles and named entity type information into the embedding layer of a transformer-based LLM will improve its factual accuracy, reduce fine-grained hallucinations, and enable more efficient inference through structured feature-based retrieval.
proposals.method: We will augment the standard embedding layer of a model like OLMo with additional, fine-tunable feature embedding tables for universal dependency relations and a coarse-grained named entity ontology (e.g., PERSON, LOCATION, DATE). For each token, its final input representation will be the sum of its standard token embedding, its syntactic role embedding (from a dependency parse), and its entity type embedding (from a pre-processing NER tagger). We will pre-train or continue pre-training this architecture on a trillion-token corpus and evaluate on hallucination benchmarks (e.g., HaluEval) and knowledge-intensive tasks (e.g., closed-book QA), comparing against a baseline with identical parameters but standard embeddings.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, linguistically-informed embedding architecture that demonstrably improves factual grounding in LLMs. It will provide empirical evidence for the benefits of explicit linguistic structure in modern neural models and create a new pathway for integrating symbolic, retrievable knowledge (like n-gram statistics from Infini-gram) directly into the model's foundational representations.
proposals.novelty: While feature-based embeddings are classic in NLP, their integration into the core embedding layer of a multi-billion parameter, transformer-based LLM for the purpose of hallucination mitigation is unexplored. This approach uniquely bridges discrete linguistic annotation with end-to-end neural training at scale, moving beyond post-hoc editing or retrieval-augmented generation.

proposals.title: Syntax-Constrained Attention: Improving Faithfulness and Efficiency in Language Models via Dependency-Guided Sparsity
proposals.motivation: Modern Transformer-based language models rely on dense, quadratic attention, which can be computationally expensive and linguistically unfaithful, allowing attention to flow across syntactically unrelated tokens. This may contribute to issues like hallucination and inefficiency. Pre-2026 work, including Hajishirzi's research on fine-grained hallucination detection and scalable n-gram models, highlights the need for more interpretable and grounded generation.
proposals.hypothesis: Constraining the Transformer's attention mechanism to respect pre-computed syntactic dependency parse trees will improve the model's factual faithfulness, computational efficiency, and interpretability without sacrificing overall language modeling performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a structured attention layer that masks attention scores to zero for token pairs not connected by a direct dependency edge or a short, predefined path in a dependency tree. This parse can be obtained from an efficient, frozen parser (e.g., a transition-based neural parser) for the input context. We will integrate this layer into decoder-only models like OLMo, fine-tuning them with a combined loss for language modeling and a syntactic consistency objective. Evaluation will use the fine-grained hallucination benchmarks from prior work and new syntactic probing tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, linguistically-informed attention mechanism that reduces the hypothesis space for next-token prediction, potentially lowering inference cost. It will provide empirical evidence on how syntactic structure impacts factual correctness, advancing the field of efficient and faithful language modeling.
proposals.novelty: While sparse attention exists, enforcing sparsity patterns based on *linguistic dependency graphs*—particularly for improving faithfulness—is under-explored. Combining this with the scaling insights from trillion-token n-gram models to create a hybrid symbolic-neural architecture is a novel direction.

proposals.title: Unified Multi-Task Learning for Factual Grounding and Semantic Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) often struggle with factual consistency, leading to hallucinations, even as they excel at semantic tasks like sentiment analysis. Pre-2026, multi-task learning (MTL) has shown promise for improving generalization, but most frameworks combine similar tasks (e.g., all QA). Jointly training on a factual, extractive task like SQuAD with diverse, non-factual tasks like sentiment analysis or NER could create a more robust internal representation that balances world knowledge with linguistic nuance, potentially mitigating hallucination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed multi-task learning framework, jointly optimizing for factual question answering (SQuAD) and semantic classification/sequence labeling tasks, will yield a model with superior factual grounding and reduced hallucination compared to single-task or homogeneous multi-task baselines.
proposals.method: We will design a transformer-based MTL framework with a shared encoder (initialized from a model like T5 or a small LLM) and task-specific decoders/heads. The training will use a dynamic gradient balancing strategy (e.g., GradNorm) to manage conflicting gradients between extractive QA (SQuAD) and classification tasks (sentiment, NLI, NER). We will leverage recent work on fine-grained hallucination detection to create a targeted evaluation suite, measuring performance on the core tasks and on out-of-domain factual reasoning benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel MTL framework specifically architected to enhance factual reliability. It will provide empirical evidence on how heterogeneous task mixtures affect internal representations and hallucination propensity. The resulting model and analysis will offer a path toward more trustworthy, general-purpose NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: While MTL is well-studied, its application to directly combat hallucination by pairing a factual knowledge-intensive task with semantically rich but non-factual tasks is novel. The integration of fine-grained hallucination metrics as a core evaluation, inspired by Hajishirzi's recent work, provides a precise lens to measure the framework's success beyond standard accuracy.

proposals.title: Infini-gram for Data-Efficient Adaptation: A Study of Sparse, Retrieval-Augmented Fine-tuning for Few-Shot QA
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) require extensive fine-tuning data for optimal task adaptation, which is costly and impractical for specialized domains. The recent Infini-gram model, a scalable, unbounded n-gram index over a trillion tokens, offers a fundamentally different, non-parametric approach to language modeling. This project investigates whether this architecture's explicit, massive-scale memory of text can be leveraged for superior few-shot or zero-shot question-answering (QA) adaptation, potentially reducing reliance on dense parameter updates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Infini-gram model's explicit, retrievable token-sequence statistics will enable more sample-efficient adaptation to new QA tasks than standard parametric LLMs, as its performance is grounded in direct evidence from its training corpus rather than purely learned parametric patterns.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Infini-gram framework for QA by designing a retrieval-augmented inference pipeline. For a given query, the system will retrieve and score relevant n-gram sequences from the trillion-token index to construct or rank candidate answers. We will then introduce a novel, sparse fine-tuning method that updates only a small set of retrieval scoring parameters or prompt embeddings, rather than the full model. We will benchmark this against few-shot fine-tuning of dense models like OLMo on datasets such as SQuAD and TriviaQA, measuring performance as a function of available training examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical analysis of a next-generation n-gram architecture's adaptability, contributing a new methodology for sparse, retrieval-based fine-tuning. It will yield concrete insights into the trade-offs between parametric learning and massive-scale non-parametric memory for data-efficient NLP.
proposals.novelty: While retrieval-augmented generation exists, applying it to an unbounded n-gram model of this scale for controlled adaptation studies is novel. The core idea of 'fine-tuning' such a model by only adjusting retrieval mechanisms, rather than model weights, presents a non-obvious path to efficiency.

proposals.title: Task Vectors from Inference: Zero-Shot Skill Acquisition via Contrastive Activation Steering
proposals.motivation: Task arithmetic, which modifies model behavior by adding task-specific 'vectors' derived from fine-tuned checkpoints, is computationally expensive and requires curated datasets. This limits its application to tasks where high-quality supervised data is available. A method to derive effective task vectors without any fine-tuning would democratize model steering, enabling rapid, data-efficient adaptation of large language models (LLMs) to new tasks and preferences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that task vectors can be constructed directly from a base model's internal activations during inference on a small set of task exemplars, by contrasting activations for correct vs. incorrect or task-specific vs. generic outputs, and that applying these vectors will steer the model's behavior effectively without parameter updates.
proposals.method: We will build on activation steering and contrastive learning techniques. For a given task, we will prompt a frozen base LLM (e.g., OLMo) with a few examples. We will extract and compare activation patterns (e.g., from later transformer layers) between runs that produce desired outputs and runs that produce generic or incorrect outputs, using a method inspired by contrastive representation learning. The resulting differential activation pattern, averaged across examples and layers, forms the 'inference-derived task vector'. This vector will then be added to forward passes via a steering mechanism like Inference-time Intervention to bias future generations.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, compute-efficient paradigm for task-specific model control that entirely bypasses fine-tuning. It will contribute a rigorous framework for constructing and evaluating zero-shot task vectors, potentially integrated with scalable, n-gram-based analysis (like Infini-gram) for pattern validation. The findings could significantly lower the barrier for safe, post-deployment model editing and hallucination mitigation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work edits models via fine-tuned weights or manually crafted activation patches, our approach is novel in algorithmically synthesizing steering vectors purely from a base model's inference-time behavior, eliminating the need for any gradient-based training or task-specific checkpoint.

proposals.title: Compositional Task Arithmetic: A Systematic Study of Vector Composition for Multi-Task Learning
proposals.motivation: Recent work on 'task vectors'—obtained by subtracting a pre-trained model's weights from its fine-tuned version—has shown that simple arithmetic (e.g., addition) can combine skills. However, this approach is largely heuristic and unexplored for composing many diverse tasks, which is crucial for building generalist models. A principled understanding of how to combine, scale, and interfere with multiple task vectors is needed to enable efficient and controllable multi-task learning without catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimal multi-task performance from composed task vectors can be achieved through a learned, non-linear composition function that accounts for task interference and semantic relatedness, outperforming naive linear addition.
proposals.method: We will systematically evaluate linear (addition, averaging) and non-linear (learned MLP, attention-based) composition functions on a diverse set of 20+ NLP tasks. Using a base model like T5 or Llama 2, we will create a library of task vectors. We will introduce a 'composition controller' network that takes multiple task vectors and a natural language task descriptor as input to predict optimal weight deltas. Performance will be benchmarked against multi-task fine-tuning and adapter-based methods, with analysis using Infini-gram for corpus-level pattern analysis of outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical framework for task vector composition, yielding practical algorithms for building multi-task models. It will contribute a diagnostic suite for understanding task interference in weight space and establish best practices for 'editing' models with combined capabilities, advancing beyond single-task fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While task arithmetic exists, a systematic study of composition for *many* tasks and the proposal of a learned, conditioned composer is novel. This bridges the gap between discrete task vectors and the continuous, conditioned adaptation needed for complex, multi-faceted instruction following.

proposals.title: TaskBank: A Scalable System for Managing and Retrieving Thousands of Task Vectors
proposals.motivation: Task arithmetic has emerged as a promising method for model editing and multi-task adaptation by adding and subtracting fine-tuning deltas. However, current methods operate on a handful of tasks, lacking the infrastructure to manage a large, growing library. Scaling to thousands of tasks is crucial for creating generalist models that can dynamically compose skills, but it introduces critical challenges in storage, interference, and efficient retrieval that remain unaddressed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing a structured, compressed representation for task vectors and a retrieval mechanism based on task descriptions, we can scale task arithmetic to thousands of tasks with minimal interference and efficient composition.
proposals.method: We will first develop a compression and indexing scheme for task vectors, building on low-rank adaptation (LoRA) and quantization techniques to reduce storage. We will then design a retrieval system that maps natural language task descriptions (or few-shot examples) to the relevant vector(s), potentially using a learned embedding model. Finally, we will introduce a composition controller, inspired by mixture-of-experts routing, to manage interference when combining many vectors, evaluating on a newly constructed benchmark of thousands of fine-tuning tasks derived from datasets like SuperGLUE and P3.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first scalable system for large-scale task vector management, a benchmark for multi-task composition, and new insights into the geometry and interference of task vectors in high-dimensional spaces. It will enable practical, dynamic model adaptation from a vast skill library.
proposals.novelty: While prior work introduces task arithmetic, it does not address the systems and retrieval challenges of scaling. Our approach uniquely combines model compression, semantic retrieval, and interference-aware composition into a unified framework for thousands of tasks.

proposals.title: Direct Task Vector Inference: Meta-Learning Parameter Shifts from Few-Shot Examples
proposals.motivation: Recent work on task vectors—the difference between a fine-tuned model and its base version—has shown promise for model editing and multi-task composition. However, obtaining these vectors currently requires full fine-tuning, which is computationally expensive and data-intensive for each new task. An efficient method to infer task vectors directly from a few examples would enable rapid, lightweight model adaptation and open new avenues for analyzing task representations in language models.
proposals.hypothesis: The parameter shift (task vector) required for a language model to perform a new task can be predicted directly from a small set of in-context examples using a meta-learned inference network, bypassing the need for gradient-based fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We propose a meta-learning framework where a hypernetwork (a small transformer encoder) is trained to predict a task vector for a target LLM (e.g., Llama 2 or OLMo) given a support set of k examples. The training data will be constructed from a diverse set of tasks (e.g., from Super-NaturalInstructions) by computing ground-truth task vectors via LoRA fine-tuning. The hypernetwork will be optimized to minimize the L2 distance between the predicted vector and the fine-tuned vector, while also maximizing the target model's performance on the task. We will leverage recent scaling techniques for n-gram analysis (e.g., Infini-gram) to inform the selection of semantically rich support examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel paradigm for few-shot adaptation, moving from in-context learning to in-context parameter inference. It will provide a fast, resource-efficient alternative to fine-tuning for new tasks and contribute a new methodology for analyzing the relationship between data geometry and parameter space shifts in large models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses task vectors post-hoc, we propose to infer them directly from data without any gradient updates to the main model. This approach is distinct from both prompt tuning and standard meta-learning, focusing on predicting the precise parameter edit rather than learning an initialization or a prompt.

proposals.title: InverseVQA: Training Multimodal Models for Inverse Visual Question Generation from Answers and Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models excel at answering visual questions but struggle with the inverse task: generating a coherent visual question given an answer and a reasoning path. This capability is crucial for educational content creation, dataset augmentation, and probing model understanding. Addressing this inverse problem can serve as a stringent test of a model's compositional reasoning and its grasp of the implicit constraints linking visual scenes, questions, and answers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model fine-tuned on a curated dataset of (answer, reasoning, visual context, question) tuples can learn to generate valid and diverse visual questions, and that this inverse generation task will serve as a powerful fine-grained hallucination detector for standard VQA models.
proposals.method: We will construct a dataset by pairing images from VQA datasets with their QA pairs, using a strong language model (e.g., GPT-4) to generate a formalized reasoning chain for each. We will then train a multimodal encoder-decoder architecture (e.g., a Flan-T5 or LLaMA variant with a vision encoder) using a sequence-to-sequence objective to generate the question conditioned on the image, the textual answer, and the reasoning path. We will leverage constrained decoding techniques and evaluate using n-gram based metrics (inspired by Infini-gram) for novelty and human evaluation for correctness.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce and benchmark a novel 'inverse' multimodal task, provide a new dataset for inverse visual question generation, and demonstrate how the task can be used to audit and edit hallucinations in standard VQA models, extending fine-grained hallucination detection to the multimodal domain.
proposals.novelty: While prior work generates questions from images, generating them from a *textual answer and reasoning path* is a novel, more constrained formulation. The application of this inverse task as a diagnostic tool for hallucination detection in multimodal models is also unexplored.

proposals.title: WiSE-FT++: Generalizing Weight-Space Ensembles for Robust Fine-tuning of Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning large pre-trained models on downstream tasks risks catastrophic forgetting and overfitting to narrow task distributions, compromising robustness and generalization. The WiSE-FT method, introduced for vision-language models, elegantly mitigates this by interpolating between pre-trained and fine-tuned weights, but its efficacy and optimal application for decoder-only large language models (LLMs) remain unexplored. Extending this investigation is crucial for developing more reliable and broadly capable LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generalized WiSE-FT framework, adapted for the architectural specifics and training dynamics of large autoregressive language models, will systematically improve out-of-distribution robustness and calibration across diverse NLP tasks without sacrificing in-domain performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the WiSE-FT algorithm for decoder-only LLMs (e.g., OLMo, Llama 2), experimenting with layer-wise and parameter-type-specific interpolation schemes beyond a single mixing coefficient. The core experiment will fine-tune base models on a suite of tasks (e.g., reasoning, dialogue, instruction-following) and create interpolated checkpoints across the weight-space trajectory between the pre-trained and fine-tuned states. We will rigorously evaluate these ensembles on in-domain, out-of-domain, and challenge datasets (e.g., for hallucination or negation), comparing against standard fine-tuning and other ensemble baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive analysis and practical framework for applying weight-space ensembles to large autoregressive language models. It will deliver concrete guidelines and a toolkit for achieving more robust fine-tuning, directly contributing to the development of more reliable and generalizable NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: While WiSE-FT is established for CLIP, its application to billion-parameter decoder-only LLMs is novel and non-trivial, requiring architectural considerations beyond simple linear interpolation. This proposal uniquely bridges robust fine-tuning methods from vision with core challenges in modern LLM development, such as hallucination mitigation.

proposals.title: Distilling Ensemble Diversity: Knowledge Distillation for Fusing Zero-Shot and Fine-Tuned Model Weights
proposals.motivation: Large language models exhibit a trade-off: zero-shot models possess broad, general knowledge but lag on specific tasks, while fine-tuned models excel on their target domain but can overfit and lose generality. Deploying an ensemble of both is computationally prohibitive. An efficient method to consolidate their complementary strengths into a single model is needed for scalable and robust NLP applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A knowledge distillation framework that treats a combined ensemble of a base model and its fine-tuned variants as a unified teacher can produce a single, more parameter-efficient student model that retains the general capabilities of the zero-shot model while incorporating the specialized performance of the fine-tuned experts.
proposals.method: We will leverage a task-arithmetic framework to create a fused teacher model by linearly combining the weights of a base model (e.g., OLMo) and several fine-tuned LoRA adapters. This fused teacher's predictions across a diverse, task-agnostic corpus (potentially indexed using Infini-gram for efficient n-gram retrieval) will then be used to distill knowledge into a smaller student model of the same architecture. The distillation loss will combine standard output distribution matching with a contrastive objective on intermediate layer representations to preserve the teacher's internal knowledge structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical algorithm for creating a single model that balances generality and specialization, reducing inference cost compared to ensembles. It will also provide analysis on the transferability of fine-tuned knowledge via weight-space fusion and distillation, contributing to the understanding of model merging.
proposals.novelty: While model merging and distillation exist separately, this proposal uniquely uses a weight-fused ensemble as the distillation teacher, directly targeting the zero-shot/fine-tuned capability gap. It moves beyond distilling black-box outputs to distilling the knowledge embedded in a constructed weight-space combination.

proposals.title: UniRep: A Non-Textual Intermediate Representation for Unified Task Inference
proposals.motivation: Current language models unify diverse tasks (e.g., reasoning, tool use, multimodal grounding) by casting them all into a textual input-output format. While flexible, this text-centric paradigm may be suboptimal, as it forces all computation and intermediate states into a sequential, human-readable form, potentially introducing inefficiencies and compounding errors. Exploring a learned, non-textual intermediate representation could streamline cross-task knowledge transfer and internal computation, improving efficiency and robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: A compact, structured, and task-agnostic intermediate representation, learned jointly across multiple modalities and reasoning tasks, will enable more parameter- and data-efficient unification than a purely textual output space, leading to improved inference-time performance and reduced hallucination.
proposals.method: We will design a model architecture with a shared encoder that projects diverse inputs (text, code, structured data) into a fixed-dimensional 'UniRep' vector space. A lightweight, multi-headed decoder will map from this space to various output modalities (text, actions, edits). Training will involve a multi-task objective on datasets spanning reasoning (e.g., MATH), code generation, and hallucination detection/editing (inspired by Hajishirzi's prior work). We will benchmark against text-only unified models (like T5 variants) and ablate the contribution of the intermediate representation using metrics from Infini-gram for coherence and fine-grained hallucination detection for faithfulness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for or against the necessity of text as a universal 'thought' language in AI systems. It will contribute a novel architecture for task unification and a diagnostic framework for evaluating the efficiency of internal representations. The findings could inform the design of next-generation, more efficient LMs.
proposals.novelty: While intermediate representations exist in modular systems, proposing a *learned*, *non-textual* representation as the core unification layer for a monolithic model challenges the dominant end-to-end text-to-text paradigm. It directly tests the assumption that text generation is the optimal unified output.

proposals.title: Instruction-Guided Discovery of Non-Linear Feature Fusion Operators from Unpaired Data
proposals.motivation: Current neural architectures for vision and language tasks rely on hand-designed, fixed patterns for multi-scale feature fusion (e.g., dilated convolutions, attention heads), which may not be optimal for diverse downstream tasks. Learning custom fusion operators typically requires extensive paired supervised data, limiting adaptability and data efficiency. This work addresses these limitations by proposing a paradigm where the fusion mechanism itself is learned end-to-end, guided only by high-level task instructions.
proposals.hypothesis: A model can learn a task-adaptive, non-linear feature fusion operator directly from unstructured natural language instructions and a stream of unlabeled, unpaired data, by optimizing for internal consistency with the instruction's semantics across diverse inputs.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-encoder architecture: a vision encoder extracts multi-scale features from an input image, and a language encoder processes a task instruction (e.g., 'segment cats'). A trainable, parameterized fusion module, initialized as a generic graph neural network over feature maps, will combine the visual features. The core training uses a contrastive objective on a large corpus of unpaired (image, instruction) data, pulling the fused representation closer to the instruction embedding and pushing it away from mismatched instructions. We will leverage pre-2026 foundation models (e.g., CLIP, ALIGN) to provide initial alignment signals and use techniques from unsupervised representation learning and our recent work on fine-grained hallucination detection to ensure semantic consistency.
proposals.contribution: This will introduce a new method for learning data-efficient, task-specific fusion operators without paired supervision, reducing reliance on curated datasets. It will provide a framework for more adaptive neural network components that can be specialized via natural language. The work will also yield insights into the interplay between high-level instructions and low-level feature composition in multimodal models.
proposals.novelty: This departs from the standard of using fixed fusion patterns or learning them from paired data, instead learning the operator from unpaired instructions via a consistency objective. It connects instruction following, typically a high-level reasoning task, to the fundamental low-level operation of feature fusion within a network's backbone.

proposals.title: Learning Dynamic Computational Structures: Task-Driven Specialization of Dilation Rates in ESP Networks
proposals.motivation: Current architectures for instruction-following, while powerful, often operate as monolithic black boxes, making it difficult to understand how they organize and apply different types of reasoning. The ESP (Efficiently Scalable Production) module, with its structured dilated convolutions, offers a promising inductive bias for hierarchical processing, but its dilation rates are typically fixed or coarsely tuned. We hypothesize that a model's internal computational structure should adapt dynamically to task demands, a principle not yet explored at scale.
proposals.hypothesis: Training an ESP-based network with per-module, learnable dilation rates on a massively diverse set of tasks (e.g., Super-NaturalInstructions) will cause an emergent, interpretable organization where specific dilation patterns specialize for clusters of functionally similar tasks, revealing how the architecture encodes task knowledge.
proposals.method: We will extend the ESP architecture by parameterizing the dilation rate for each convolutional layer as a continuous, learnable variable, optimized via gradient descent alongside standard network weights. The model will be trained on a unified version of the Super-NaturalInstructions dataset, encompassing hundreds of distinct task types. We will employ analysis techniques from recent work on interpretability and module analysis (e.g., probing, activation clustering) to correlate learned dilation patterns with task clusters identified via human-defined or data-driven taxonomies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel method for learning dynamic, context-sensitive network architectures; 2) empirical evidence for emergent task specialization within model components; and 3) a step towards more interpretable and modular systems that explicitly represent acquired skills in their computational structure, moving beyond mere performance benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored dynamic architectures (e.g., mixture of experts) or fixed multi-scale processing, the idea of learning *dilation geometry* as a direct, interpretable representation of task knowledge from a diverse curriculum is novel. It uniquely uses task diversity not just for generalization, but as a curriculum for architectural self-organization.

proposals.title: Instruction-Conditioned ESPNet: Dynamic Spatial Pathway Modulation for Efficient Vision-and-Language Task Execution
proposals.motivation: Deploying capable vision-language models on edge devices for real-time tasks like robotic control requires architectures that are both computationally efficient and precise in following complex instructions. Current methods often treat language instructions as a static conditioning signal, failing to achieve the fine-grained, dynamic control needed for spatial tasks. There is a critical need for models that deeply integrate linguistic semantics with visual processing in a parameter- and compute-efficient manner.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel 'Instruction-to-Spatial-Pathway' (ISP) module, which uses encoded instructions to dynamically modulate the filters and connections within the hierarchical spatial pathways of an efficient ESPNet-like backbone, will enable superior accuracy in instruction-guided vision tasks while maintaining the efficiency required for edge deployment.
proposals.method: We will scale and adapt the ESPNet architecture, integrating a pre-trained language encoder (e.g., from a compact LM like a distilled T5). The core ISP module will project encoded instruction features into a set of dynamic convolutional filters and gating weights, which will be injected at multiple stages of the ESPNet encoder to condition spatial feature extraction. We will train the system end-to-end on a multi-task blend of instruction-following datasets (e.g., referring expression comprehension, robotic manipulation from language, interactive image editing) using a combination of task-specific and instruction-fidelity losses, drawing on recent fine-grained hallucination detection techniques for supervision.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, dynamically modulated neural architecture for efficient vision-and-language reasoning, 2) empirical evidence on scaling efficient backbones for instruction following, and 3) a strong baseline model for edge-device deployment in interactive AI applications, evaluated on precise task execution metrics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond simple feature concatenation or attention-based fusion to a mechanism of instruction-driven, dynamic architectural modulation within an efficient convolutional network's spatial pathways, a concept not explored in prior efficient vision-language work.

proposals.title: Attention-Guided Multi-Granularity Knowledge Distillation for Compressing Contextual Priors into Efficient Segmentation Networks
proposals.motivation: Efficient semantic segmentation models like ESPNet are crucial for edge deployment but suffer from a limited receptive field, hindering their ability to model long-range scene dependencies. Standard knowledge distillation often fails to transfer these critical contextual priors from large teacher models, leading to a performance gap on complex, structured scenes. This work addresses this receptive field mismatch to enable efficient, yet contextually aware, segmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly guiding a compact student network (ESPNet) to mimic a large teacher's long-range contextual relationships, using attention-weighted distillation at multiple representation granularities, will yield a student model with significantly improved robustness and accuracy while maintaining efficiency.
proposals.method: We design an attention-guided, multi-granularity distillation framework. Beyond output logits, we distill intermediate feature maps and novel affinity matrices capturing pairwise token relationships from the teacher's transformer blocks. Spatial and channel attention modules, derived from the teacher's self-attention maps, dynamically weight the distillation loss to force the student to prioritize learning the teacher's most critical global structural dependencies. The student ESPNet architecture is augmented with lightweight auxiliary layers to receive and process these guided signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new distillation methodology specifically tailored for transferring contextual knowledge to efficient vision architectures. It will produce a publicly available, robust ESPNet variant that closes the accuracy gap with large models on structured datasets like ADE20K. The framework provides a principled approach to compressing scene understanding priors into efficient networks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard feature distillation, our method explicitly transfers long-range structural knowledge via teacher-derived attention maps and affinity matrices, directly targeting the receptive field limitation. The multi-granularity, attention-guided weighting mechanism for contextual priors is a novel extension of distillation techniques prevalent in NLP to dense prediction tasks.

proposals.title: ESPNet-KD: Knowledge Distillation for Efficient Semantic Segmentation via Text-Guided Feature Alignment
proposals.motivation: Deploying high-performance semantic segmentation models on edge devices is critical for real-time applications like autonomous systems, but is constrained by computational resources. While compact models like ESPNet offer efficiency, they often sacrifice accuracy compared to large, state-of-the-art models. Knowledge distillation (KD) provides a pathway to bridge this performance gap, but standard KD methods for segmentation often fail to effectively transfer the rich, structured spatial knowledge from teacher to student.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distilling knowledge from a large, powerful segmentation model (e.g., a Mask2Former variant) into a compact ESPNet student via a novel, text-conditioned feature alignment loss will significantly boost the student's accuracy without increasing its inference-time complexity.
proposals.method: We will use a large, pre-trained segmentation model as a static teacher. The core innovation is a distillation loss that aligns the intermediate feature maps of the ESPNet student with those of the teacher, guided by textual embeddings of class labels derived from the teacher's own internal language-aware representations (inspired by vision-language model insights). This goes beyond standard logit-matching by enforcing semantic and spatial consistency. Training will involve a combined loss: the standard segmentation cross-entropy loss for the student and the proposed text-guided feature distillation loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a highly efficient yet accurate version of ESPNet suitable for edge deployment. It will contribute a new KD framework for dense prediction tasks that leverages textual semantics for improved feature transfer. We will provide extensive benchmarks on datasets like Cityscapes and ADE20K, demonstrating a new state-of-the-art for model efficiency in segmentation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating text-guided alignment, drawing from multimodal reasoning, into the distillation process for a purely visual task, moving beyond existing feature imitation methods. This approach specifically tailors the transferable knowledge to be semantically relevant, which is non-obvious for standard segmentation KD pipelines.

proposals.title: ESPNet-IT: Discovering Generalist Subnetworks via Instruction-Tuned Pruning
proposals.motivation: Efficient architectures like ESPNet are crucial for on-device AI, but their capacity limits are typically evaluated on narrow benchmarks, not on broad task generalization. The expanded NATURAL INSTRUCTIONS dataset provides a diverse, instruction-based task suite, offering a unique testbed for measuring true generalization. Determining how to prune such models while preserving cross-task performance is an open problem with significant implications for deploying capable, compact models.
proposals.hypothesis: Iteratively pruning ESPNet while fine-tuning on the diverse NATURAL INSTRUCTIONS task distribution will force the model to retain a sparse, task-agnostic subnetwork, whose performance degradation provides a direct empirical map between parameter efficiency and generalization breadth.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a pre-trained ESPNet model on the full NATURAL INSTRUCTIONS v2 dataset. We will then apply an iterative magnitude pruning algorithm, where after each pruning cycle (e.g., removing 10% of remaining weights with smallest magnitude), the model is further fine-tuned on the same instruction mix. This process continues until performance on a held-out validation set of diverse tasks drops below a pre-defined threshold. The final sparse model and the trajectory of performance vs. sparsity will be analyzed.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel methodology for evaluating the intrinsic generalization limits of efficient architectures. It will produce a highly compressed, generalist ESPNet model and a quantitative analysis of the trade-off between sparsity and robust multi-task performance, guiding future efficient model design.
proposals.novelty: While pruning and instruction tuning exist separately, using a massive, diverse instruction set as the *criterion* for iterative pruning to discover a generalist subnetwork is novel. This fuses the objectives of architectural efficiency and cross-task generalization in a single, task-aware process.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Gold Standard: Quantifying Noise, Bias, and Inconsistency in Human-Annotated Factual Verification Benchmarks
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of language models for factual accuracy and hallucination detection relies heavily on human-annotated datasets as the gold standard. However, pre-2026 work in annotation theory and studies of inter-annotator disagreement suggest human judgments are inherently noisy and subjective. This creates a flawed foundation for model development, where improvements may reflect better alignment with imperfect human biases rather than genuine gains in factual reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: Human annotations for factual verification tasks contain systematic, quantifiable noise and bias that, when modeled, reveal the benchmarks themselves are unreliable, and that models trained to be robust to this annotation noise will generalize more effectively to unseen factual claims.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a large-scale re-annotation study on existing fact-checking datasets (e.g., FEVER) to measure inter- and intra-annotator inconsistency. We will then introduce a novel training and inference framework where the model learns a distribution over possible human judgments for a given claim-evidence pair, rather than a single gold label. This builds on Hajishirzi's group's expertise in fine-grained editing and inference, using a latent variable model to separate factual signal from annotator-specific noise. Performance will be evaluated on a newly curated 'adversarial' test set where human disagreement is high.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic taxonomy and quantification of error types in human factual verification. It will yield a new model training paradigm that is robust to annotation noise and a more reliable benchmark for evaluating factual accuracy, moving the field beyond a simplistic reliance on human judgments as ground truth.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes human disagreement, this proposal actively models it as a core component of training and challenges the very use of a single 'gold' label, directly contrasting with standard supervised learning approaches for fact verification. It leverages scaling laws from Infini-gram and fine-grained analysis from hallucination editing to dissect the annotation process itself.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Factuality: A Systematic Analysis of Model Size, Training Data, and Factual Precision
proposals.motivation: While scaling language models improves performance on many tasks, its impact on factual precision is not well understood. Hallucinations remain a critical barrier to trustworthy AI. This work aims to empirically establish how factuality, a core component of reliability, scales with model parameters and training data, informing future model development and deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that factuality, as measured by metrics like FActScore, improves predictably with model scale and training data diversity, but that this relationship plateaus or becomes non-monotonic for certain factual domains, revealing specific scaling inefficiencies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled study across a suite of decoder-only LMs (e.g., OLMo, Pythia, GPT-NeoX) of varying scales. Using the FActScore framework, we will generate and evaluate long-form biographies from these models. We will correlate factuality scores with model size (parameters), training tokens (using datasets like Dolma), and data mixture proportions. We will introduce fine-grained analysis using Infini-gram's suffix array indices to trace the provenance of correct and incorrect facts back to specific training data segments.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first comprehensive scaling laws for factuality, identifying which scaling dimensions most improve factual precision. It will contribute a diagnostic toolkit linking hallucinations to training data artifacts. The findings will guide strategies for efficient, factual model training and inform the development of better hallucination detection and editing methods.
proposals.novelty: Prior scaling laws focus on loss and broad capabilities; this work uniquely targets factual precision. The integration of unbounded n-gram analysis (Infini-gram) for data attribution within a scaling study provides a novel, fine-grained lens on the origins of factual errors.

proposals.title: Memorization in Implicit Factual Recall: A Fine-Grained Analysis of Generative Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Recent work on memorization and hallucination in language models (LMs) has largely focused on explicit, verbatim recall or open-ended generation. However, in real-world tasks like question answering (QA), factual recall is implicit and interwoven with reasoning and generation. Understanding how memorization manifests in this integrated setting is crucial for developing more reliable and trustworthy LMs, yet it remains an underexplored area.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in generative QA, LMs exhibit distinct, fine-grained patterns of memorization that differ from explicit recall tasks, and that these patterns can be systematically measured and edited using scalable n-gram analysis to improve factual fidelity.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Infini-gram framework to analyze the trillion-token training corpus of models like OLMo, tracing the provenance of fact-bearing n-grams in model-generated QA outputs. Building on fine-grained hallucination detection methods, we will develop a new metric to quantify 'implicit memorization' by correlating n-gram novelty with answer correctness. We will then design a targeted editing intervention that modifies model inference based on the memorization profile of retrieved n-grams, aiming to suppress low-confidence memorized fragments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of memorization in integrated generation tasks, yielding a new diagnostic benchmark for implicit factual recall. It will also contribute a novel inference-time editing method that uses memorization signals to reduce hallucinations in QA, advancing towards more controllable and factual generation.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely combines scalable, corpus-based memorization analysis (Infini-gram) with fine-grained hallucination editing, applying it to the nuanced setting of generative QA where factual recall is implicit, a significant departure from prior work on explicit retrieval or verbatim copying.

proposals.title: TailBench: A Scalable Framework for Generating Long-Tail Factual Benchmarks to Stress-Test Model Memorization
proposals.motivation: Current factual QA benchmarks like PopQA and EntityQuestions, while valuable, are skewed towards popular entities, failing to probe the true long-tail memorization limits of large language models (LLMs). As models scale, understanding whether their knowledge expands proportionally into the long tail or merely deepens for head entities is critical for assessing their reliability and potential for hallucination in real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that even state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit a sharp performance drop on factual queries involving entities and relations beyond a specific frequency threshold in their training data, and that this drop is not adequately captured by existing benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Infini-gram index, a trillion-token n-gram corpus, to systematically identify entity-relation pairs with precisely quantified, low occurrence frequencies. Using this data, we will generate a scalable benchmark, TailBench, with fine-grained frequency bins. We will then conduct a rigorous evaluation of models like OLMo, analyzing performance degradation across bins and employing fine-grained hallucination detection techniques to categorize error types. The benchmark construction will be automated to allow for periodic updates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel, scalable benchmark for evaluating long-tail factual knowledge, 2) A detailed analysis of the relationship between training data frequency and model recall, and 3) New insights into failure modes (e.g., confabulation vs. omission) in the long tail, directly informing future work on hallucination editing and robust training.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior benchmarks that sample based on entity popularity, TailBench uses a massive n-gram index to programmatically construct test sets based on exact token-sequence frequencies, enabling unprecedented precision in targeting the knowledge frontier of LLMs.

proposals.title: Inference from Fragments: Training Language Models to Execute Tasks from Partial and Multi-Modal Cues
proposals.motivation: Current instruction-following models require complete, well-formed textual instructions, limiting their applicability in real-world scenarios where instructions are ambiguous, truncated, or conveyed through non-textual cues (e.g., a pointing gesture or an incomplete command). This gap hinders seamless human-AI collaboration. Addressing it requires moving beyond the paradigm of processing only complete textual inputs.
proposals.hypothesis: Language models can be trained to robustly infer and execute intended tasks from incomplete or multi-modal instruction cues by jointly learning to reconstruct the latent, full instruction and perform the correct action, improving robustness and user experience.
proposals.method: We will extend a pre-trained LM (e.g., OLMo) with a training framework that introduces "instruction erosion" during fine-tuning. For a dataset of (full instruction, task) pairs, we will create synthetic examples by randomly masking spans of the instruction text or replacing them with learned embeddings representing abstract multi-modal cues (e.g., [POINT], [HIGHLIGHT]). The model will be trained with a multi-task objective: (1) to perform the original task (e.g., QA, editing) and (2) to reconstruct the masked instruction fragments, leveraging scalable n-gram indexing (e.g., Infini-gram) for efficient retrieval of plausible completions during training. Hallucination detection metrics will be used to evaluate the fidelity of the model's inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new training paradigm for making instruction-following more robust and flexible. It will provide a benchmark and model capabilities for handling incomplete or multi-modal instructions, directly advancing towards more intuitive human-AI interfaces. The integration of task performance with instruction reconstruction offers a novel evaluation lens.
proposals.novelty: While prior work detects hallucinations in model outputs, we proactively train models to handle and reason with inherently incomplete or ambiguous inputs. The method uniquely combines instruction infilling with task execution in a single fine-tuning framework, diverging from standard instruction-tuning which assumes textual completeness.

proposals.title: Task-Scaffolding for Emergent Instruction Following: Can Strategic Task Selection Replace Scale?
proposals.motivation: Current large language models achieve strong instruction-following through massive multi-task training on datasets like FLAN and Super-NaturalInstructions. This paradigm is computationally expensive and obscures whether generalization stems from the sheer volume of tasks or the strategic relationships between them. Understanding the minimal, high-quality task scaffolding required for emergent instruction-following is crucial for more efficient and interpretable model training.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strong zero-shot instruction-following can emerge from training on a very small, carefully curated set of tasks if those tasks are selected to maximally scaffold compositional reasoning and diverse output formats, rather than from exposure to thousands of tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a "task-scaffolding" algorithm that selects a minimal seed set from a large pool like P3 or NI. This algorithm will optimize for graph-theoretic properties like coverage of reasoning primitives (e.g., deduction, extraction, formatting) and output schema diversity. We will then fine-tune a base model like OLMo or T5 on this minimal set and evaluate its zero-shot performance on held-out instructions from BIG-Bench Hard and novel user-oriented prompts. We will compare against models trained on randomly selected task sets of the same size and against standard instruction-tuned models, using both automated metrics and fine-grained hallucination detection methods to analyze failure modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for data-efficient instruction tuning, potentially reducing the data requirements for robust generalization. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating the compositional generalization of instruction-tuned models. The findings will offer insights into the fundamental mechanisms of instruction-following, distinguishing between benefits from task diversity and from underlying reasoning skill acquisition.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales the number of tasks, we invert this approach to seek a minimal sufficient set, framing task selection as an optimization problem for meta-skill acquisition. This directly challenges the prevailing assumption that "more tasks" is the primary driver of generalization in instruction tuning.

proposals.title: Instruction-Driven Self-Training: Learning from Unlabeled Data via Consistency and N-gram Verification
proposals.motivation: Current instruction-tuning of large language models (LLMs) relies heavily on costly, human-curated input-output pairs, creating a scalability bottleneck. This limits the ability to adapt models to new domains and tasks efficiently. A method to leverage the vast amounts of unlabeled text and simple task instructions would democratize and accelerate the specialization of LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model can learn to follow instructions by generating its own training data from unlabeled corpora and refining its outputs through self-consistency and factual grounding checks, eliminating the need for pre-paired supervision.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage self-training framework. First, given an instruction and a retrieved context from a massive unlabeled corpus (leveraging trillion-token n-gram indexes like Infini-gram for efficient retrieval), the model generates multiple candidate outputs. Second, we apply a fine-grained verification module, inspired by hallucination detection work, to score candidates based on internal consistency and n-gram based factual alignment with the source context. High-scoring (input, output) pairs are used for iterative fine-tuning. The base model could be a robust, general-purpose LLM like OLMo.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide a novel, scalable pipeline for instruction-tuning without paired data, reducing dependency on human annotation. It would advance our understanding of self-supervised task learning and offer a new methodology for domain adaptation. The integration of n-gram verification for self-training data filtering is a novel contribution to improving output reliability.
proposals.novelty: While self-training exists, its application to instruction-following without any seed paired data is unexplored. The key novelty is the closed-loop of instruction, generation, and verification using scalable n-gram statistics and fine-grained hallucination detectors to create a high-quality training signal autonomously.

proposals.title: Synthetic Instruction Refinement: A Data-Centric Framework for Enhancing Instruction-Tuning Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is crucial for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but scaling high-quality, human-authored instruction datasets is costly and can introduce noise. Recent work, including Hajishirzi's on fine-grained hallucination detection, highlights the need for precise, high-fidelity training data. This project investigates whether we can systematically generate or refine instruction data using models themselves to create more effective training corpora than raw human crowdsourcing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop system, where an LLM critiques and refines existing crowdsourced instructions, can produce a synthetic dataset that yields superior instruction-following performance and data efficiency when used for fine-tuning compared to the original human data.
proposals.method: We will leverage a strong base LLM (e.g., OLMo) and the Infini-gram framework for efficient, trillion-token-scale retrieval of relevant contexts. The core method is a three-step iterative pipeline: 1) Use a critic model, trained on fine-grained error taxonomies from prior hallucination work, to identify weaknesses (e.g., ambiguity, lack of constraint) in instructions from datasets like Super-NaturalInstructions. 2) Generate refined instruction variants via in-context learning, using Infini-gram to retrieve exemplars of high-quality instructions. 3) Fine-tune a target model on the refined set and evaluate its performance on held-out human evaluation and benchmark tasks (e.g., BBH, instruction-following fidelity). We will ablate the source of refinement (model vs. human) and measure the data efficiency curve.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable methodology for instruction dataset curation and a publicly released refined instruction set. It will provide empirical evidence on the relative value of human vs. model-refined data for alignment, advancing data-centric AI for language models.
proposals.novelty: While data filtering and augmentation are common, the novelty lies in systematically using a model's own critique and generation capabilities, grounded in scalable n-gram statistics and fine-grained error detection, to *improve upon* human crowdsourced data for the specific objective of instruction tuning.

proposals.title: Scaling Task Diversity: A Trillion-Token Benchmark for Instruction Generalization and Hallucination Analysis
proposals.motivation: Benchmarks like NATURAL INSTRUCTIONS have driven progress in instruction following, but their task diversity remains limited, potentially overestimating model generalization. Concurrently, large language models exhibit fine-grained hallucinations that are difficult to detect and correct. A systematic investigation into how scaling task diversity affects both generalization performance and the nature of model errors is critically needed to understand the true limits of current paradigms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that massively expanding the task diversity in instruction-tuning data will reveal a performance plateau and a predictable shift in error types, where failures of factual recall are supplanted by systematic compositional and reasoning hallucinations, measurable via n-gram-based novelty detection.
proposals.method: We will construct a new benchmark, 'Diverse-Instruct', by programmatically generating and curating tasks from a trillion-token corpus using the Infini-gram index to ensure novel, low-probability n-gram sequences, pushing beyond templated data. We will fine-tune and evaluate the OLMo model family on this benchmark. Hallucination analysis will employ a two-stage detector: first, Infini-gram will flag low-support text spans; second, a fine-grained editor, trained on datasets from prior Hajishirzi work, will classify and correct error types (e.g., entity fabrication vs. logical contradiction).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) a large-scale, diversity-focused benchmark for rigorous generalization testing, 2) a detailed taxonomy of how error profiles evolve with task complexity, and 3) an integrated framework linking scalable n-gram statistics with fine-grained hallucination editing for model diagnosis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic use of an unbounded n-gram model (Infini-gram) for both benchmark *creation* (to maximize genuine novelty) and *evaluation* (for hallucination detection), directly connecting scaling laws for task diversity with the emergence of specific, detectable failure modes.

proposals.title: InstructGram: Enhancing Instruction-Following with Scalable N-gram Memory and Fine-Grained Hallucination Control
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in instruction-tuned LLMs, they often fail to follow complex, multi-step instructions precisely, leading to omissions or hallucinations. This limits their reliability in critical applications like task automation and reasoning. Current architectures lack explicit mechanisms to decompose instructions and ground their execution in verifiable, factual patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting a transformer-based instruction-following model with a scalable, unbounded n-gram memory index and a fine-grained hallucination detection/editing module will significantly improve its precision, factual consistency, and ability to execute complex instructions.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture, InstructGram, built upon a strong base model (e.g., OLMo). First, we will integrate an Infini-gram index over a trillion-token corpus as a fast, external memory, enabling the model to retrieve and verify high-likelihood factual sequences during instruction decomposition. Second, we will incorporate a fine-grained hallucination detector, trained on synthetic and human-annotated data, to identify and edit speculative or ungrounded content in the model's step-by-step reasoning before final output generation. The training will involve multi-task learning on instruction-following, retrieval-augmented generation, and hallucination editing objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new model architecture with built-in mechanisms for instruction verification and hallucination mitigation. It will provide empirical evidence on the utility of massive-scale n-gram statistics for improving instruction fidelity. The resulting model and benchmarks will advance the development of more reliable and controllable AI assistants.
proposals.novelty: While n-gram models and hallucination detection exist separately, their synergistic integration into a single instruction-following architecture is novel. This approach uniquely uses scalable, non-parametric memory not just for knowledge retrieval, but for real-time validation of instruction decomposition steps against observed textual patterns.

proposals.title: TaskWeaver: Dynamic Multi-Task Instruction Tuning with Gradient-Aware Task Grammars
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning enables large language models (LLMs) to follow diverse human instructions, but current methods often train on mixtures of tasks with static, uniform sampling. This ignores the complex inter-task relationships and varying learning dynamics, potentially leading to suboptimal performance, negative interference, and inefficient training. A principled strategy for dynamically scheduling and blending tasks during instruction tuning is a critical open problem for building robust, generalist models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling task relationships via a learnable, gradient-based "task grammar" and using it to dynamically adjust multi-task training curricula will significantly improve instruction-following accuracy and reduce catastrophic interference compared to standard uniform mixing.
proposals.method: We will introduce TaskWeaver, a training framework that first uses a lightweight probe to compute per-task gradient signatures during initial training phases. These signatures will be used to construct a Task Affinity Graph, quantifying gradient alignment and conflict. During main training, we will employ a dynamic scheduler that samples task batches based on real-time loss trends and the affinity graph, prioritizing tasks that are currently learnable and minimizing gradient interference. We will evaluate on a diverse suite of instruction-following benchmarks, comparing against static mixing and prior curriculum learning methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically-grounded method for multi-task instruction tuning that explicitly manages gradient conflicts. It will deliver a scalable open-source framework for dynamic task scheduling and offer new insights into the structure of task manifolds in LLMs, advancing towards more sample-efficient and robust instruction tuning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on task weighting or curriculum learning often relies on heuristics like task complexity or loss, our approach is novel in directly leveraging gradient-based task grammars to model interference and guide real-time sampling, a fine-grained strategy not yet explored for large-scale instruction tuning.

proposals.title: Infini-Task: Scaling Instruction-Tuning to 500k Tasks for Systematic Generalization Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current instruction-tuned models are trained on a few thousand tasks, limiting our understanding of how scaling task diversity affects generalization. The emergence of new capabilities in large language models (LLMs) is often unpredictable and poorly understood. A systematic, large-scale study of task and instruction scaling is needed to probe the boundaries of systematic generalization and compositional reasoning.
