proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling instruction-tuning to hundreds of thousands of procedurally generated, compositionally structured tasks will lead to predictable, quantifiable improvements in systematic generalization, moving beyond the plateau observed with smaller, curated task sets.
proposals.method: We will leverage the OLMo 3 architecture as a base model. Using a grammar-based framework inspired by Infini-gram's scaling principles, we will programmatically generate a corpus of 500,000+ unique tasks with formal instruction templates. Tasks will be designed with controlled compositional structures (e.g., varying logical operators, linguistic constructs, and domain knowledge). We will fine-tune OLMo 3 on this corpus and employ rigorous evaluation using held-out task families and a novel fine-grained hallucination detection metric to analyze generalization patterns versus memorization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the largest-scale empirical analysis of task scaling to date, establishing new benchmarks for systematic generalization. It will yield a publicly released model and task generation framework, enabling future research on data composition and curriculum design for instruction following.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales data tokens (Infini-gram) or detects hallucinations, this proposal uniquely scales *task schema* count by orders of magnitude using procedural generation to directly test the 'task scaling hypothesis' for systematic generalization, a previously unexplored axis.

proposals.title: One-Task Instruction Tuning: Probing the Limits of Cross-Task Generalization in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning has become a dominant paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but it typically requires massive, diverse datasets of hundreds of tasks. This raises a fundamental question about the nature of the acquired capability: does performance on unseen tasks stem from learning to follow instructions as a general skill, or from extensive exposure to many task formats? Resolving this is crucial for understanding model generalization and for developing more efficient, data-lean alignment methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model, when instruction-tuned on a single, carefully chosen task with high compositional complexity, can exhibit significant zero-shot generalization to a broad spectrum of unseen tasks by learning a core 'instruction-following' primitive, rather than merely memorizing task-specific patterns.
proposals.method: We will adapt the T5 or OLMo architecture as a base model. The core experiment involves fine-tuning on only one instructional dataset (e.g., a subset of Natural Instructions focused on 'text editing' or 'question decomposition'). We will rigorously evaluate zero-shot performance across standard benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BIG-Bench Hard, Super-NaturalInstructions). Crucially, we will employ fine-grained hallucination detection and editing techniques from Hajishirzi's prior work to analyze failure modes and whether errors stem from instruction misunderstanding or knowledge gaps. We will contrast this with models tuned on multiple tasks and ablate task choice using n-gram analysis from Infini-gram to quantify the linguistic diversity of the single training task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a lower bound on the data required for effective instruction following, challenging the necessity of massive multi-task collections. It will yield a diagnostic framework for what makes a task compositionally generative for instruction learning. The findings could lead to more efficient model specialization and a clearer mechanistic understanding of how models generalize from instructions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies scaling laws for multi-task instruction tuning, systematically isolating the effect of a *single* task to probe the emergence of general instruction-following is novel. The integration of fine-grained hallucination analysis and large-scale n-gram statistics to diagnose the generalization mechanism moves beyond mere performance reporting.

proposals.title: Inverse Instruction Induction: Recovering Task Definitions from Model Behavior via Contrastive N-gram Guidance
proposals.motivation: Current instruction-tuning and alignment methods operate in a forward direction: a human writes an instruction, and the model learns to follow it. This makes the quality and scalability of alignment dependent on human-authored data. An inverse method, which can automatically infer the underlying instruction or intent from observed input-output behavior, would enable the automated curation and refinement of instruction datasets, improve our ability to audit and interpret model capabilities, and provide a novel pathway for self-improving systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model, guided by a scalable, retrieval-augmented n-gram index of its own outputs, can be trained to perform robust inverse instruction induction—generating the precise instruction that explains a set of (input, output) demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will frame inverse instruction induction as a sequence-to-sequence generation task. Given a few (input, output) pairs from a latent task, a T5 or Llama-based model will be trained to generate the corresponding instruction. Training will leverage a novel contrastive objective: for a given set of demonstrations, the model must generate an instruction that scores highly under a retrieval-augmented verifier. This verifier will use the Infini-gram index to compute the pointwise mutual information between the generated instruction and the provided outputs, contrasting it with outputs from other tasks. We will fine-tune on a synthetically constructed corpus of tasks (e.g., from Super-NaturalInstructions) and their demonstrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel benchmark and evaluation protocol for inverse instruction induction, 2) a new training methodology combining generative models with large-scale n-gram retrieval for contrastive verification, and 3) analysis showing how recovered instructions can be used for fine-grained hallucination detection by identifying gaps between induced intent and generated content.
proposals.novelty: While prior work infers latent concepts or edits model behavior, the explicit formulation of instruction generation from demonstrations as a contrastive, retrieval-augmented learning problem is novel. It uniquely leverages unbounded n-gram statistics (Infini-gram) not for next-token prediction, but for verifying the semantic alignment between a candidate instruction and observed behavior.

proposals.title: Infini-gram Guided Instruction Tuning: Data-Efficient Alignment for Sub-100M Parameter Models
proposals.motivation: The scaling law paradigm has driven NLP toward massive models, creating a deployment and accessibility gap. While instruction tuning enables powerful, controllable models like those in the OLMo family, its effectiveness for models under 100M parameters remains poorly understood. Enabling capable, instruction-following tiny models is crucial for on-device applications, efficient inference, and democratizing access to aligned AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that leveraging a trillion-token, retrieval-augmented n-gram index (Infini-gram) to curate and augment a highly diverse, high-quality instruction-tuning dataset will enable sub-100M parameter models to achieve instruction-following capabilities previously requiring models an order of magnitude larger.
proposals.method: We will first use the Infini-gram index to perform large-scale, fine-grained analysis of existing instruction datasets (e.g., Alpaca, ShareGPT) to identify and filter out low-quality or hallucination-prone examples. We will then use the index for retrieval-augmented generation to synthesize new, high-diversity instruction-response pairs targeting identified gaps. We will train and evaluate a series of compact architectures (e.g., distilled OLMo variants, efficient transformers) under 100M parameters on this curated dataset, benchmarking instruction-following performance, truthfulness, and efficiency against zero-shot and few-shot baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel methodology for data-centric, efficient model alignment and establish a new performance benchmark for instruction-following in the sub-100M parameter regime. It will contribute a public, high-quality, Infini-gram-verified instruction-tuning dataset specifically designed for tiny models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that scales up data and model size, we scale down the model by scaling up data intelligence, using a massive n-gram index not for direct generation but for rigorous dataset curation and augmentation—a novel application of retrieval-based methods for efficient alignment.

proposals.title: Dual: Evaluating and Improving the Pedagogical Efficacy of Machine-Generated Instructions for Human Learning
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate instructions, tutorials, and procedural knowledge for humans. However, their ability to produce pedagogically effective instructions, as opposed to just fluent text, is not well understood. This gap is critical as reliance on AI for education and task guidance grows, necessitating a formal evaluation of how well humans can learn from machine-authored content.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current LLM-generated instructions suffer from systematic pedagogical deficiencies (e.g., missing prerequisite steps, poor sequencing) that measurably impair human learning efficiency and task performance compared to expert human-authored instructions, and that these deficiencies can be automatically detected and edited for improvement.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled human-subject study where participants learn novel tasks (e.g., using a software tool, following a crafting recipe) from instructions authored by a state-of-the-art LLM (like OLMo) or a human expert. We will measure learning outcomes via task completion speed, accuracy, and post-test questionnaires. Concurrently, we will analyze the instruction sets using fine-grained hallucination detection methods and the Infini-gram framework to identify statistical anomalies, coherence breaks, and factual inconsistencies that correlate with poor human performance. This analysis will inform the development of an automatic instruction editor model, fine-tuned to correct identified pedagogical flaws.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, empirical benchmark for the pedagogical quality of machine-generated instructions. It will yield a diagnostic framework linking textual flaws in LLM output to human cognitive outcomes and demonstrate a proof-of-concept system for automatically refining instructions to enhance human learnability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work evaluates LLM output for factuality or coherence, this proposal uniquely frames instruction generation as a human-in-the-loop learning problem, applying Hajishirzi's expertise in fine-grained text analysis and scaling n-gram methods to model the pedagogical structure of instructions, not just their surface form.

proposals.title: CRITIC-SYNTH: Learning Self-Critique Tokens via Synthetic Feedback and N-gram Guided Consistency
proposals.motivation: Training language models to generate internal critique tokens (e.g., [critique], [fact-check]) for self-reflection and hallucination reduction currently relies on costly, high-quality human-annotated data, limiting scalability and accessibility. This bottleneck hinders the development of robust, generalizable reflection capabilities in models, especially for long-tail or evolving knowledge domains where annotation is impractical. A method to learn effective critique generation from weaker supervision is a critical open problem in creating more reliable and self-aware language models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model can learn to generate accurate, actionable internal critique tokens by training on a curriculum of synthetic feedback, where the quality of supervision is progressively refined using scalable consistency checks derived from large-scale n-gram statistics and entailment-based verifiers.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, we use a strong base model (e.g., OLMo) to generate candidate critiques and edits for its own outputs, creating a synthetic dataset. Second, we employ a filtering and refinement module that scores these examples using a combination of (1) consistency metrics from the Infini-gram index to flag improbable or contradictory spans, and (2) a lightweight entailment model trained for fine-grained hallucination detection. The refined, high-confidence examples are then used to fine-tune a model to prepend learned, task-specific critique tokens to its generations, enabling self-reflection before output.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable pipeline for learning self-critique abilities without heavy human annotation, directly advancing the field of controllable and trustworthy language generation. It will provide new insights into using large-scale, model-agnostic statistics (n-grams) as weak supervisors for training meta-cognitive model behaviors. The resulting models and code will be released to facilitate further research in self-improving language models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic use of trillion-token n-gram statistics (Infini-gram) as an automatic, scalable consistency filter for synthetic critique data, moving beyond purely model-based verification. This approach uniquely combines weak supervision from massive corpora with fine-grained entailment models to bootstrap a capability (self-critique) that has previously required strong human supervision.

proposals.title: Scaling Self-Reflective Retrieval-Augmented Generation to Trillion-Parameter Language Models
proposals.motivation: The Self-RAG framework, which interleaves retrieval, critique, and generation, has shown promise for improving the factuality and controllability of language models. However, its evaluation has been largely confined to models under 70B parameters (e.g., Llama-2). As the field pushes toward frontier models with hundreds of billions of parameters (e.g., GPT-4, Claude, and open models like OLMo), it is unknown whether the benefits of Self-RAG scale effectively or if the inherent knowledge and reasoning of massive models diminish the utility of such structured self-reflection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying the Self-RAG framework to language models with hundreds of billions of parameters will yield disproportionate improvements in factuality and attribution on knowledge-intensive tasks, as the model's stronger base reasoning capabilities will enable more accurate self-critique and selective retrieval than in smaller models.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Self-RAG architecture for models like the 405B-parameter OLMo 3. The implementation will involve fine-tuning the base LM to generate special reflection tokens that trigger retrieval, critique (relevance, support), and revise actions. We will leverage scalable, dense retrieval indices built from trillion-token corpora, informed by Infini-gram's scaling insights. Evaluation will be conducted on fine-grained hallucination benchmarks and long-form QA tasks, comparing the scaled Self-RAG's performance against the base LM and standard RAG baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of how self-reflective RAG techniques perform at the scale of frontier models. It will deliver an open, scalable Self-RAG implementation for a massive open model and establish whether the cost of iterative retrieval and critique is justified by measurable gains in attribution and reduced hallucination for state-of-the-art LMs.
proposals.novelty: While Self-RAG is established, its application and rigorous evaluation on models with hundreds of billions of parameters is unexplored. The novelty lies in testing the interaction between a model's vast parametric knowledge and its ability to orchestrate external retrieval, a non-obvious synergy that could redefine efficient deployment of frontier LMs.

proposals.title: Critique-Opt: Direct Preference Optimization for Hallucination Detection and Correction
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate plausible but non-factual text, a critical issue for trustworthy AI. While recent work has introduced fine-grained hallucination detection and editing, these critique models are typically trained via supervised fine-tuning on static datasets, which may not align with nuanced human judgments of factuality and helpfulness. Optimizing critique generation directly for human preferences remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to a model's internal critique module will directly optimize its ability to identify and correct hallucinations in a manner that aligns with human preferences, leading to more accurate and useful model outputs.
proposals.method: We will build upon a fine-tuned hallucination detection/correction model (e.g., based on T5 or a decoder-only LLM). We will train a separate reward model on human preference data comparing pairs of critique-and-edit outputs. Using this reward model, we will apply Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) or Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to the critique-generation policy. The policy's action space will be the generation of a critique token sequence, which then conditions the final text editing step.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel framework for aligning critique generation with human preferences, moving beyond static supervision. We will release a new dataset of human preferences on critique quality and an optimized model that demonstrates improved performance on fine-grained hallucination benchmarks like HaluEval, measured by both detection F1 and the factual correctness of edited outputs.
proposals.novelty: While RLHF has been used to align general text generation, its application to optimize a model's *internal self-critique and correction mechanism* is novel. This approach directly targets the reasoning process behind hallucination mitigation rather than the final output alone.

proposals.title: Infini-RAG: Scaling Self-Retrieval to a Trillion-Token, Multi-Modal Corpus for Factual Language Generation
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves factual accuracy in language models, but existing systems rely on small, homogeneous, and often static corpora, limiting their knowledge scope and robustness. Scaling the retrieval corpus to billions of documents across text, code, and structured data in multiple languages presents fundamental challenges in efficient indexing, relevance scoring, and cross-modal grounding that must be solved for next-generation, trustworthy AI assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a scalable, trillion-token n-gram index (like Infini-gram) with a fine-grained, multi-vector retriever and a Self-RAG-style critic, we can create a language model that dynamically retrieves from a massive, heterogeneous corpus to significantly improve factuality and reduce hallucinations across diverse tasks and languages.
proposals.method: We will construct a corpus of billions of web documents, books, code repositories, and tabular data, pre-processed into a unified token stream. For retrieval, we will extend the Infini-gram engine for fast, unbounded n-gram lookups and pair it with a dense retriever (like Contriever) for semantic search. The model will be based on a fine-tuned LLM (e.g., OLMo) with a Self-RAG framework, where a critic module learns to trigger retrieval and judge the relevance of retrieved passages. Training will involve fine-grained hallucination detection data to teach the critic when and what to retrieve.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a scalable architecture for indexing and searching trillion-token, multi-modal corpora, 2) a new state-of-the-art model for open-domain QA and long-form generation with demonstrably lower hallucination rates, and 3) a comprehensive analysis of how corpus scale and heterogeneity affect RAG performance across modalities and languages.
proposals.novelty: While Self-RAG introduces learned retrieval, and Infini-gram scales n-gram indexes, no work has combined a learnable retrieval policy with a truly web-scale, multi-modal n-gram/dense hybrid index, creating a system that leverages both precise lexical matches and deep semantic search across an unprecedented knowledge base.

proposals.title: QueryGen: Training Language Models for Inverse Retrieval by Generating Queries from Passages
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is critical for grounding large language models (LLMs) in factual knowledge, but its performance hinges on retrieving the most relevant passages given a user query. This creates a fundamental asymmetry: models are optimized for retrieval *from* queries, not *for* queries. A model that can generate the likely queries for a given passage can directly improve retrieval systems by enabling synthetic training data generation, optimizing dense retriever training through contrastive learning, and providing a novel lens for analyzing and debugging hallucination in generative models by identifying what knowledge a passage does *not* support.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model, fine-tuned on (passage, query) pairs with a controlled, passage-conditioned generation objective, can produce diverse and plausible user queries that would trigger the retrieval of that passage, and these generated queries can be used to improve downstream retrieval and hallucination detection tasks.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a T5 or encoder-decoder LLM (e.g., T5-XXL) on existing retrieval corpora (e.g., MS MARCO, Natural Questions) to perform inverse mapping: given a passage as input, generate multiple possible queries. We will introduce a fine-grained training objective that combines a standard likelihood loss with a retrieval consistency reward, where a frozen retriever (e.g., DPR or Contriever) must successfully retrieve the original passage given the generated query. To ensure diversity and quality, we will incorporate techniques from controlled text generation, such as conditional training with query type prefixes and nucleus sampling. Performance will be evaluated by measuring the retrieval recall of generated queries and through human evaluation of query plausibility and specificity.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce and benchmark a new 'inverse retrieval' task, providing a publicly available model and dataset for query generation from passages. It will demonstrate concrete utility by showing how generated queries can augment training data for dense retrievers, improving their robustness, and serve as a probe for fine-grained hallucination detection by identifying query-passage mismatches.
proposals.novelty: While prior work generates questions from documents for QA, our focus is on generating diverse, open-ended *retrieval queries*—which are often shorter, more varied, and less formal—and explicitly tying the generation objective to the retrieval task's success signal. This bridges Hajishirzi's recent themes on fine-grained hallucination analysis and scalable n-gram methods (for evaluating query commonness) with core problems in training and inference for retrieval systems.

proposals.title: Jointly Syntactic-Semantic Parsing via Latent Graph Synchronization for Enhanced Language Model Interpretation
proposals.motivation: Current NLP pipelines treat syntactic parsing and semantic role labeling (SRL) as separate, sequential tasks, leading to error propagation and a loss of mutual constraints that could improve accuracy. A unified model for joint parsing is crucial for complex language understanding tasks like reasoning and hallucination detection, where both structure and meaning must be coherently resolved. This aligns with the need for more interpretable and reliable models, as highlighted by recent work on fine-grained hallucination analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained to synchronize latent syntactic and semantic graph representations within a single, multi-task architecture will outperform cascaded systems and improve the fidelity of downstream inference tasks by enforcing structural and interpretative consistency.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified encoder-decoder Transformer model with two synchronized decoders: one for generating a syntactic dependency tree and one for a semantic role graph. A novel latent synchronization layer will be introduced to allow iterative message passing between decoders during generation, using a constrained inference mechanism inspired by chart parsing. The model will be pre-trained on a combined objective of masked language modeling and next-sentence prediction, then fine-tuned on a jointly annotated corpus (e.g., the enhanced Universal Dependencies with PropBank annotations) using a multi-task loss balancing syntactic and semantic accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new state-of-the-art model for joint syntactic-semantic parsing, providing a single, efficient tool for deep text interpretation. It will contribute a novel architecture for latent graph synchronization that could be applied to other multi-structure prediction problems. The resulting parses will serve as a stronger foundation for model editing and hallucination detection by providing explicit, aligned structural analyses.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored multi-task learning for parsing, our approach of explicit, iterative latent synchronization between concurrently generated graphs is novel. It moves beyond simple parameter sharing or pipeline models to a truly joint inference process, leveraging pre-2026 advances in constrained decoding and structured prediction within Transformer frameworks.

proposals.title: Infini-Gram Augmented Synthesis: Scaling Diagram QA with Complex, Multi-Step Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current visual question answering (VQA) and diagram understanding datasets often lack questions requiring complex, multi-hop reasoning, limiting the evaluation and training of models for deep comprehension. As large language models (LLMs) advance, there is a critical need for benchmarks that stress-test their ability to integrate textual and visual information over multiple reasoning steps, a key frontier for reliable AI assistants. This gap is particularly acute for diagrammatic data, where logical, mathematical, and hierarchical reasoning is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: Leveraging a trillion-token n-gram index (Infini-gram) to guide a fine-tuned LLM in a controlled generation process will enable the scalable creation of high-quality, complex, multi-step questions for diagram QA that are more challenging and diverse than existing datasets.
proposals.method: We will first fine-tune a capable base LLM (e.g., OLMo) on existing diagram QA data and textual reasoning chains. Using the Infini-gram model as a massive-scale, unbounded n-gram index, we will implement a constrained decoding or retrieval-augmented generation pipeline to ensure the synthetic questions incorporate linguistically plausible and factually consistent multi-step reasoning templates. This method will be applied to a large corpus of diagrams, with each generated question-and-answer pair automatically validated for logical coherence and answerability against the diagram, followed by human-in-the-loop refinement for a high-quality final corpus.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a large-scale, publicly available benchmark for complex diagrammatic reasoning, pushing the frontier of multimodal evaluation. It will also contribute a novel methodology for using statistical language models at scale (Infini-gram) to control and improve the quality of synthetic data generation for challenging reasoning tasks.
proposals.novelty: While data synthesis with LLMs is common, the integration of a trillion-token n-gram index (Infini-gram) as a constraint mechanism for ensuring linguistic and reasoning plausibility in complex question generation is a novel approach. This uniquely combines Hajishirzi's recent work on scaling n-gram models and fine-grained hallucination detection to address a core data scarcity problem in multimodal reasoning.

proposals.title: Aligning Language Models via Self-Generated In-Context Instructions: A Fine-Tuning-Free Approach
proposals.motivation: Aligning large language models (LLMs) to human values and safe behaviors typically requires extensive and costly fine-tuning (e.g., RLHF, DPO). This process is resource-intensive, risks catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained knowledge, and can be brittle to distribution shifts. A method that achieves alignment without parameter updates would offer a more efficient, flexible, and knowledge-preserving alternative.
proposals.hypothesis: A model can be aligned to follow instructions and produce safe, helpful outputs purely through in-context learning, by strategically curating and presenting a sequence of self-generated instruction-response demonstrations that guide its own subsequent behavior, thereby bypassing the need for fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will leverage a large, capable base model (e.g., OLMo) to generate a diverse corpus of instruction-response pairs. Using the Infini-gram framework for efficient, trillion-token-scale n-gram analysis, we will identify and filter pairs that exhibit high semantic alignment and low hallucination (applying fine-grained detection methods). The core intervention is a dynamic in-context prompting strategy that selects and orders these self-generated exemplars to maximally steer the model's output towards desired behaviors for novel instructions, evaluated on safety and helpfulness benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for the limits and potential of in-context alignment, challenging the prevailing assumption that parameter updates are necessary. It will introduce a novel, scalable prompting methodology for behavior steering and contribute a curated dataset of self-generated alignment demonstrations for community analysis.
proposals.novelty: While in-context learning is known for task adaptation, its systematic application for *value alignment* without any fine-tuning is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in using the model's own generations, filtered via scalable n-gram analysis and hallucination detection, as the sole mechanism for behavioral alignment.

proposals.title: Ensemble-of-Experts for Instruction Generation: Improving Diversity and Quality via N-gram Guided Inference
proposals.motivation: High-quality, diverse instruction datasets are crucial for fine-tuning capable and generalist language models, but their manual creation is a bottleneck. Current methods for synthetic instruction generation often rely on single, large models, which can produce repetitive or low-quality outputs, limiting the effectiveness of subsequent fine-tuning. This problem is central to improving the robustness and task-generalization of language models, a core theme in pre-2026 NLP research.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble of specialized, smaller models, guided by a scalable n-gram index to filter and rank outputs, can generate instruction data that is more diverse and of higher quality than a single, larger monolithic model.
proposals.method: We propose to train or fine-tune a suite of smaller, architecturally diverse 'expert' models (e.g., based on OLMo variants) on distinct data domains. During generation, we use a Mixture-of-Experts style inference where prompts are routed to relevant experts. The outputs from all experts are then scored and filtered using a large-scale, trillion-token n-gram index (Infini-gram) to measure fluency and novelty, penalizing repetitive or common n-gram patterns. Finally, a lightweight verifier model, trained for fine-grained hallucination detection, will refine the selected candidates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, automated pipeline for creating superior instruction-tuning datasets. It will advance ensemble methods for text generation by integrating explicit, statistical n-gram guidance into the inference process. The resulting public dataset and framework will provide a resource for improving model instruction-following and reducing output monotony.
proposals.novelty: While ensembles are known, their application to instruction generation is underexplored. The key novelty is the integration of a massive, unbounded n-gram model (Infini-gram) as a real-time, statistical filter during ensemble inference to explicitly optimize for lexical and phrasal diversity, moving beyond simple probability-based ranking.

proposals.title: Beyond Classification: A Unified Framework for Hallucination Detection and Editing in Open-Ended Generation
proposals.motivation: Current fine-grained hallucination detection methods, like those in Hajishirzi's recent work, are primarily evaluated on classification or question-answering tasks. However, the most critical and challenging hallucinations occur in open-ended text generation (e.g., creative writing, summarization) and sequence-to-sequence tasks (e.g., translation, dialogue), where the output space is vast and unconstrained. A systematic analysis of hallucination patterns and correction mechanisms in these generative settings is lacking, limiting the real-world safety and reliability of large language models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core principles of fine-grained, evidence-based hallucination detection can be effectively generalized to open-ended generation tasks, and that targeted editing interventions based on this analysis will significantly improve the factual consistency of generated text without degrading its fluency or coherence.
proposals.method: We will extend the experimental framework from 'Fine-grained Hallucination Detection and Editing' to generative tasks. First, we will adapt the detection mechanism to use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and models like Infini-gram as a scalable, external evidence base to score the factual grounding of each generated span. For sequence-to-sequence tasks (e.g., summarization with T5 or BART), we will treat the source document as primary evidence. We will then develop and benchmark several editing strategies, including constrained decoding with the evidence base, post-hoc iterative revision using the detector as a feedback signal, and fine-tuning with a novel loss that penalizes ungrounded tokens.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark and methodology for fine-grained hallucination analysis in open-ended text generation. It will yield actionable insights into how hallucination manifests differently across task types and produce a toolkit for model editing that enhances factual accuracy in generative settings. This directly advances the goal of building more trustworthy and controllable language models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work detects hallucinations in constrained outputs, applying and validating a fine-grained, token-level detection-and-editing pipeline on unbounded generative tasks is novel. Furthermore, leveraging trillion-token n-gram models (Infini-gram) as a scalable, static knowledge source for real-time generation guidance presents a unique alternative to dynamic retrieval.

proposals.title: Robustness or Memorization? Analyzing In-Context Learning Sensitivity to Label Noise in Tiny Language Models
proposals.motivation: Recent work on large language models (LLMs) has shown a surprising robustness to random or flipped labels in few-shot demonstrations, suggesting they primarily rely on the input patterns rather than the provided supervision. However, this phenomenon has not been systematically studied in the emerging class of highly efficient, sub-100M parameter 'tiny' LMs, which are critical for on-device deployment. Understanding if this robustness scales down is essential for diagnosing whether these models perform true in-context learning or merely memorize surface patterns from pre-training.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tiny language models, due to their limited capacity, will exhibit significantly greater sensitivity to random label noise in demonstrations compared to their larger counterparts, revealing a fundamental difference in their in-context learning mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled experiments using a suite of tiny LMs (e.g., 10M-100M parameters, such as distilled versions of models like OLMo) and standard-sized baselines. We will evaluate on diverse NLP tasks (e.g., textual entailment, sentiment analysis) from benchmarks like SuperGLUE, systematically varying the proportion of corrupted labels in few-shot prompts. Performance will be measured against a 'gold-label' demonstration baseline. We will introduce a fine-grained analysis framework, inspired by hallucination detection work, to categorize error types and measure the point of breakdown for tiny LMs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical characterization of in-context learning robustness in the tiny LM regime, offering insights for efficient model design and prompting strategies. It will contribute a diagnostic benchmark for assessing whether a model's few-shot performance stems from reasoning or memorization, directly informing the safe and reliable deployment of small models.
proposals.novelty: While label noise robustness is known in large LMs, it is non-obvious how this property manifests at the extreme low end of the scale, where model capacity is severely constrained. This bridges the line of inquiry on in-context learning with the practical constraints of model efficiency and distillation.

ideas.title: Task-Aware Pruning for Generalist Models via Instruction Tuning
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using the expanded, highly diverse NATURAL INSTRUCTIONS dataset not just as a testbed, but as a pruning criterion for ESPNet. The model will be iteratively pruned while being fine-tuned on this broad task distribution, forcing it to retain only the parameters essential for cross-task generalization. This process aims to discover a maximally efficient, 'generalist' subnetwork within ESPNet whose performance degradation is measured across the expanded task suite, providing a direct empirical map between model capacity, architectural efficiency, and generalization breadth. The outcome is both a highly compressed model and a novel methodology for evaluating the intrinsic generalization limits of efficient network architectures.

ideas.title: Task-Driven Dynamic Dilation: Learning ESP Module Specialization from Diverse Instruction-Following
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training an ESP-based network with uniquely learnable per-module dilation rates on a massively expanded and diversified NATURAL INSTRUCTIONS dataset. The core hypothesis is that the pressure to solve hundreds of distinct task types will cause the network to discover an emergent, interpretable organization of its ESP modules, where specific dilation patterns become specialized for clusters of functionally similar tasks (e.g., long-context reasoning, step-by-step procedure execution, or multi-attribute comparison). This moves beyond simple performance gains, aiming to use task diversity as a curriculum for learning a dynamic, context-sensitive computational architecture where the dilation structure itself becomes a representation of acquired task knowledge.

ideas.title: Learning Non-Linear Fusion Operators from Unstructured Instructions
ideas.description: This research challenges two standard assumptions in neural network design: that feature fusion requires a fixed, decomposed convolution pattern (e.g., as in ESP modules) and that learning such operators requires paired input-output examples. Instead, we propose a system that learns a non-linear fusion operator end-to-end, guided only by high-level instructions (e.g., 'segment the foreground object') and a stream of unlabeled data. The model discovers how to combine multi-scale features in a task-relevant way by optimizing for consistency with the instruction across diverse, unpaired inputs, leading to a more adaptive and data-efficient fusion mechanism.

ideas.title: Instruction-Scaled ESPNet: A Computationally Efficient Architecture for Precise Task Execution
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel, scaled-up variant of the efficient ESPNet architecture, specifically redesigned to excel at instruction understanding and execution. The core innovation is a new 'Instruction-to-Spatial-Pathway' module that deeply fuses encoded linguistic instructions with the model's hierarchical spatial feature maps, enabling dynamic filter and pathway modulation based on the command. By scaling the network's width and depth strategically around this instruction-conditioning core, the model achieves high accuracy in parsing and executing detailed commands for tasks like robotic manipulation or interactive image editing, while maintaining the computational efficiency required for deployment on edge devices.

ideas.title: Attention-Guided Multi-Granularity Distillation for Compressing Contextual Priors into ESPNet
ideas.description: This research addresses the receptive field mismatch in standard distillation by designing a novel attention-guided framework to transfer long-range contextual knowledge. The system distills knowledge at multiple granularities—output logits, intermediate feature maps, and specially designed affinity matrices—using spatial and channel attention modules to weight the transfer. These attention maps, derived from the teacher, force the compact ESPNet student to focus on learning the teacher's most critical global structural relationships and scene dependencies. The result is a significantly more robust ESPNet model that maintains efficiency while achieving segmentation accuracy closer to large models on complex, structured scenes.

ideas.title: Micro-ESPNet for Instruction Completion from Partial Multi-Modal Cues
ideas.description: This research designs a novel micro-ESPNet architecture for microcontrollers that operates not on complete textual instructions, but on partial, multi-modal cues from its environment. The system learns to map sparse sensory inputs (like a short audio command, a specific motion pattern, or a change in a sensor value) to a full set of embedded semantic instructions that a compact vision or control model can execute. This fusion allows energy-harvesting or battery-less devices to behave adaptively and contextually without the energy cost of processing or receiving lengthy text, pushing the boundary of on-device intelligence under extreme power constraints. The core challenge is co-designing the ultra-efficient neural model and the cue-to-instruction completion mechanism to be robust and reliable with minimal parameters.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Human Visual Efficiency with ESPNet for Dense Task Instruction Following
ideas.description: This research develops a human-in-the-loop benchmark where crowdworkers perform pixel-level tasks (e.g., 'highlight all wet surfaces') on complex scenes under time constraints, establishing a human performance baseline for efficiency and accuracy. Concurrently, the ESPNet architecture is adapted and trained to perform the same instruction-following dense tasks, creating a directly comparable machine agent. The core analysis uses the efficient ESPNet backbone not just as a performer, but as a probe to dissect the trade-offs humans make between speed, accuracy, and resource allocation under instruction, thereby generating a new benchmark for 'instruction efficiency' in computer vision that moves beyond pure accuracy.

ideas.title: NAS-Distilled ESPNet: Joint Architecture Search and Knowledge Transfer for Efficient Segmentation
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that integrates Knowledge Distillation (KD) directly into the Neural Architecture Search (NAS) loop for designing Efficient Spatial Pyramid (ESP) networks. Instead of first searching for an architecture and then distilling knowledge into it, the search process evaluates candidate ESPNet variants based on their ability to effectively learn from and mimic a large, pre-trained teacher model's representations. This co-design approach allows the NAS to discover compact, hardware-aware ESPNet architectures that are fundamentally shaped for efficient knowledge transfer, resulting in student models that achieve a better accuracy-efficiency trade-off than those produced by sequential application of NAS and KD.

ideas.title: Distilled Upsampling for Efficient Segmentation
ideas.description: This research fuses learned upsampling with knowledge distillation for ESPNet by designing a multi-target distillation loss that transfers not only final segmentation logits but also intermediate feature maps and, crucially, the teacher's upsampling behavior. The student's sub-pixel convolution layers are trained to mimic the teacher's ability to reconstruct fine spatial details during decoding, guided by distillation at multiple resolutions. This approach enables the compact ESPNet to internalize high-resolution contextual reasoning from a large teacher, resulting in superior boundary accuracy and detail preservation compared to applying either technique in isolation.

ideas.title: ESPNet-Auto: Decoupled Training for Stable Adaptive Computation
ideas.description: This system introduces a training framework that separates the learning of adaptive computational policies from the learning of visual features to ensure stability. First, a weight-shared super-network encompassing all potential computational pathways (e.g., different spatial resolutions) is trained with a uniform sampling strategy to produce robust, co-adapted features. Then, a small, efficient controller network is trained via policy gradient methods to make dynamic resolution decisions, using the frozen super-network's performance (e.g., accuracy vs. latency) as a stable reward signal. This approach yields a single network capable of reliable, input-dependent adaptation without the training instability inherent in end-to-end dynamic graph learning.

ideas.title: Accuracy-Preserving NAS for Efficient Sparse Processing (ANESP)
ideas.description: This research proposes ANESP, a novel neural architecture search framework that inverts the traditional efficiency-first paradigm. The process begins by using a supernet or search space that is pre-validated to contain subnetworks capable of matching state-of-the-art accuracy on a target task. The NAS algorithm then operates exclusively within this high-accuracy subspace, applying and optimizing ESP principles—like dynamic sparsity, conditional computation, and mixture-of-experts—to discover the most efficient architectures under given computational constraints. This ensures the final compressed model is not a degraded version of an accurate model, but an accuracy-preserving, automatically discovered instantiation of efficient sparse processing.

ideas.title: Efficient Spatial Pyramid Neural Architecture Search (ESP-NAS) for Task-Aware Dense Prediction Backbones
ideas.description: This research proposes ESP-NAS, a framework that overcomes the rigidity of a fixed ESPNet backbone by formulating efficient dense feature extraction as a neural architecture search problem. The search space is constructed using composable variants of the ESP module, allowing the discovery of backbone architectures tailored to specific dense prediction tasks like depth estimation or medical image segmentation. The NAS process optimizes a combined loss balancing task accuracy and on-device latency, ensuring the final model is both high-performing and efficient. This results in a family of task-specialized, efficiency-optimized backbones that generalize the principles of ESPNet more effectively than a single, static architecture.

ideas.title: Dynamic Resolution Knowledge Distillation for Adaptive ESPNet
ideas.description: This research fuses knowledge distillation with dynamic network design to create a novel training paradigm for efficient segmentation. A large, static teacher model is used to distill knowledge into a single ESPNet-based student architecture that is trained to operate effectively at multiple internal resolutions and widths. This enables the student to dynamically adjust its computational cost during deployment based on input complexity or device constraints, without needing multiple specialized models. The core synergy is that the distillation loss acts as a consistent, high-quality supervisory signal across all student sub-networks, ensuring robust performance at every possible operating point of the single dynamic model.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Based Multi-Scale Training with Progressive Difficulty
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that dynamic input resolution during training should be implemented with random variability from the start. Drawing an analogy from Motor Skill Acquisition in Sports Science, where athletes use a progressive curriculum of simplified tasks to build robust skills, the adapted solution is a Curriculum-Based Multi-Scale Training with Progressive Difficulty for ESPNet. The training is structured in distinct phases: Phase 1 uses only low-resolution inputs to allow the network to learn stable, fundamental visual features efficiently. Phase 2 gradually introduces higher resolutions in a scheduled manner, increasing the complexity of the spatial details to be processed. The final phase employs stochastic, dynamically varying resolutions within each batch, mimicking the unpredictable conditions of deployment. This progressive escalation of difficulty, mirroring an athletic training regimen, is hypothesized to yield more robust and generalizable feature representations than a purely random dynamic resolution approach from the outset.

ideas.title: ESPNet-Scale: A Unified Scaling Framework for Compound Model Efficiency
ideas.description: This research proposes ESPNet-Scale, a novel framework that unifies and extends the ideas of aggressive scaling and architectural exploration. Instead of independently scaling depth or width, it systematically studies the compound scaling of ESPNet's depth, width, and input resolution under a constrained floating-point operations per second (FLOPs) budget for server-side deployment. The core synergy is developing automated scaling policies—inspired by but distinct from neural architecture search—that discover optimal scaling dimensions (e.g., deeper and moderately wider) to maximize accuracy for a given latency target, effectively closing the gap with larger models while preserving the core efficiency principles of the ESPNet family. This results in a family of models, not just a single variant, each optimally configured for specific server-side accuracy-efficiency Pareto points.

ideas.title: Sparse Task-Conditioned Routing for Efficient Multi-Task Vision on Edge Devices
ideas.description: This system replaces a rigid shared backbone with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) style architecture built for edge deployment. A compact, learnable router analyzes the input and the specified task to dynamically select and combine only a sparse subset of specialized feature experts within the network for each forward pass. This design minimizes negative transfer by isolating task-specific computations while maintaining parameter efficiency, as the full set of experts is never fully activated. The model is trained end-to-end with a routing loss that encourages both task performance and sparsity, resulting in a single deployable model that can perform multiple vision tasks with near-specialist accuracy and latency suitable for resource-constrained hardware.

ideas.title: Assume a Shared Biochemical Pathway for Multi-Task ESPNet
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a standard multi-task learning backbone is optimal for edge deployment by drawing an analogy from systems biology. In metabolic engineering, a single, efficient core biochemical pathway (e.g., glycolysis) is used to produce multiple compounds by adding specific enzymatic branches. Adapting this, we propose to architect the ESPNet backbone explicitly as a shared 'core pathway' of convolutional layers designed for maximal feature reuse with minimal latency. Lightweight, task-specific 'branch modules' (small convolutional heads) are then engineered to diverge from specific points in this core, like enzymatic branches, to perform segmentation and other vision tasks concurrently. This structured modularity, inspired by biological efficiency, aims to optimize the trade-off between shared computation and task-specific performance for resource-constrained edge devices.

ideas.title: NAS-Driven Dynamic-Resolution ESPNet for Runtime-Aware Adaptive Efficiency
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that integrates Neural Architecture Search (NAS) directly into the design of a dynamic-resolution ESPNet. Instead of using NAS to find one fixed optimal ESP configuration, the search space is defined to include adaptive mechanisms—such as the number of parallel branches, their dilation rates, and filter sizes—that can be triggered based on a real-time assessment of input spatial complexity and computational load. The NAS process is then constrained by a multi-objective reward balancing accuracy, latency, and energy consumption across a simulated spectrum of device states and input types. The outcome is a single, lightweight ESPNet model embedded with a learned controller that dynamically selects from a pre-searched, Pareto-optimal set of sub-architectures at runtime, achieving superior efficiency across diverse edge scenarios compared to static or heuristically adaptive models.

ideas.title: Branch-Attentive ESPNet: Integrating Micro-SE Modules Within Parallel Dilated Convolutions
ideas.description: This idea enhances the ESP (Efficient Spatial Pyramid) module by embedding lightweight, branch-specific squeeze-and-excitation (SE) attention within its parallel dilated convolutional structure. Instead of applying a single SE block to the fused output, a micro-SE module is inserted into each parallel branch to re-weight the channels of that branch's feature maps independently. This design respects the heterogeneous, multi-scale nature of ESP by allowing each branch to adaptively emphasize its most informative channels based on the input, prior to fusion. The result is a more expressive and robust multi-scale feature representation that maintains efficiency while directly addressing the shortcomings of a monolithic, post-fusion attention mechanism.

ideas.title: Resolution-Adaptive ESP Networks with Dynamic Receptive Field Learning
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel ESPNet variant trained with a variable input resolution strategy, where each unique ESP module in the hierarchy is not only architecturally distinct but also learns resolution-specific dilation patterns. During training, the network is exposed to a wide range of input scales, forcing each module to specialize its receptive field configuration for the contextual information most relevant at that network depth and for that input resolution. This co-adaptation of module structure and training scale produces a model that is not merely robust to resolution changes, but one whose internal feature representations are explicitly optimized for multi-scale perception. The result is a single, highly efficient network capable of superior performance across a continuous spectrum of input sizes without manual redesign or post-hoc scaling.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Enforced Resolution Invariant Feature Learning for ESPNet
ideas.description: This system trains ESPNet with a paired-batch strategy where each mini-batch contains the same images at two different, randomly sampled resolutions. A consistency loss (e.g., KL divergence or mean squared error) is applied between the intermediate feature maps of these paired inputs, forcing the network to learn resolution-agnostic representations. The training follows a curriculum that starts with a narrow resolution range and progressively expands it to a wide spectrum, ensuring stable learning. Finally, an adversarial discriminator is optionally used to further encourage the feature distributions from different resolutions to be indistinguishable.

ideas.title: Attention-Guided Adaptive Dilation in Heterogeneous ESP Networks
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel architecture where each ESP module in a network learns a unique set of dilation rates, breaking the constraint of identical replication. Crucially, these heterogeneous modules are integrated with a shared spatial-channel attention mechanism that operates across the network. The attention maps dynamically weight the importance of different spatial features and scales, guiding which unique dilation patterns in subsequent modules should be emphasized or suppressed for a given input. This synergy allows the network to adapt its effective receptive field structure in a context-sensitive manner, optimizing multi-scale feature extraction for complex visual tasks beyond what static or independently learned modules could achieve.

ideas.title: Joint Architectural Search for Pareto-Optimal Accuracy-Efficiency Frontiers
ideas.description: This research designs a unified framework that treats accuracy and efficiency as co-primary objectives from the initial design phase. It employs a neural architecture search (NAS) mechanism guided by a multi-objective reward function that directly balances task performance (accuracy) against computational metrics (e.g., FLOPs, parameter count, latency). The system discovers a family of models along the Pareto frontier, revealing architectures inherently efficient without sacrificing accuracy, rather than compressing a bulky model post-hoc. This results in models where efficiency is a core property, not a compromised afterthought.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Coherent Dilation Policies for ESP Networks
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses architectural incoherence by learning unique per-module dilation rates under a unifying meta-policy. A lightweight controller network or a differentiable policy sampler observes the network's state and predicts dilation configurations that are globally coherent, ensuring receptive fields form a valid hierarchy. The policy is trained jointly with the main network weights via gradient-based meta-learning, optimizing for both task performance and architectural stability. This results in a dynamically optimized, multi-scale architecture where diversity in dilation is beneficial rather than disruptive.

ideas.title: Learned Non-Linear Fusion via Neural ODEs for Efficient Spatial Pyramid Networks
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption in Efficient Spatial Pyramid (ESP) modules that a standard convolution must be decomposed into pointwise and depthwise separable convolutions for efficiency. Drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), where fixed equation decompositions are being superseded by learned Neural ODE solvers, we adapt this solution to the computer vision context. The analogous CFD solution replaces handcrafted, linear separations of fluid dynamics equations with end-to-end learned, non-linear Neural ODEs that capture complex interactions more efficiently. Adapted to this research, we propose to replace the ESP's decomposed convolution blocks with a lightweight Neural ODE block. This block would learn a continuous, non-linear transformation for fusing multi-scale features, directly integrating contextual information without being bound to the pre-defined decomposition structure. The goal is to discover if a learned, holistic fusion operator can be more parameter-efficient and performant than the incumbent decomposed approach, fundamentally questioning the necessity of the decomposition assumption in efficient network design.

ideas.title: Scaling ESPNet via Heterogeneous Branching inspired by Metabolic Network Modularity
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from metabolic engineering, where complex networks are scaled not uniformly but by targeted reinforcement of bottleneck modules. The core research is to develop a deeper or wider ESPNet variant to close the accuracy gap with larger models. The adapted approach involves first analyzing the existing ESPNet's Hierarchical Spatial Pyramid (HSP) modules to identify specific dilation branches or fusion points that act as performance bottlenecks. Inspired by the analogous solution, we then propose to scale the network heterogeneously by selectively widening (adding more filters or parallel convolutional streams) or deepening (adding more blocks) only these critical, identified branches. This strategy aims to yield a more significant accuracy gain per unit of added computational cost compared to a naive uniform scaling of depth or width across all network components, leading to a more efficient and powerful ESPNet variant.

ideas.title: Adaptive Signal Filtering for ESP via Biological Receptor Desensitization
ideas.description: The original idea proposes adding a squeeze-and-excitation (SE) block to the ESP module for channel-wise attention. Drawing an analogy from systems biology, specifically cellular receptor desensitization mechanisms, we adapt that solution. In biology, cells dynamically down-regulate their response to persistent signals to remain sensitive to new information. The adapted idea implements an adaptive, dynamic channel gating mechanism within the ESP fusion stage. This mechanism computes not just global average pooling but also a measure of activation consistency for each channel, using it to re-weight features. Channels with high but constant activations (akin to background biological noise) are dampened, while channels with more variable or contextually specific activations are enhanced. This results in an ESP module that performs context-aware, adaptive feature filtering, potentially improving performance on tasks where distinguishing salient features from consistent background is crucial.

ideas.title: EfficientNet-inspired Adaptive Dilation Networks
ideas.description: This research adapts the EfficientNet compound scaling approach to ESP module design in convolutional networks. The analogous problem in neural architecture search was how to create optimal families of related but non-identical network architectures. The solution was compound scaling with learnable coefficients. Adapted to ESP networks, this means designing a mechanism where dilation rates across ESP modules follow a learnable scaling pattern (e.g., geometric progression with learnable ratio), creating modules that are unique but systematically related. This maintains architectural coherence while allowing each module to specialize for different receptive field requirements, optimizing the trade-off between parameter efficiency and representational capacity through gradient-based learning rather than manual tuning or random assignment.

ideas.title: Learned Upsampling via Sub-Pixel Convolution for Semantic Segmentation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from digital signal processing, where learned or designed filter banks are used for high-quality audio upsampling, to the problem of upsampling in semantic segmentation networks. The analogous problem is reconstructing a high-fidelity, high-resolution signal (audio or image features) from a lower-resolution representation without introducing artifacts. The adapted solution replaces the simple, fixed bilinear upsampling layers in ESPNet with learned sub-pixel convolution layers. These layers function as a trainable, multi-channel filter bank that learns an optimal spatial and channel-wise combination of the low-resolution feature maps to produce sharper, more accurate high-resolution semantic maps, directly transferring the principle of learned reconstruction filters from signal processing to computer vision.

ideas.title: Intrinsic Attention-Gated ESP (IAG-ESP) for Efficient Dense Prediction
ideas.description: This idea proposes an Intrinsic Attention-Gated ESP (IAG-ESP) module that embeds attention directly into the fabric of the ESP block's operation without adding separate computational layers. It leverages the multi-scale feature maps already produced by the ESP's parallel dilated convolutions to compute lightweight spatial importance maps and channel-wise scaling factors. These factors act as dynamic gates to selectively emphasize or suppress information flow from each branch before concatenation, enhancing feature discrimination. The design strictly maintains the parameter and FLOP efficiency of the original ESP module, making it suitable for real-time mobile vision applications.

ideas.title: Sample-Efficient, Physics-Constrained Neural Architecture Search for Robust ESP Design
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the sample inefficiency and poor generalization of vanilla NAS by embedding domain knowledge directly into the search process. A physics-informed simulator acts as a low-fidelity filter, pruning architecturally valid but physically non-viable ESP configurations from the vast search space. A meta-learned predictor, trained on a combination of simulated and sparse real hardware measurements, then guides a multi-objective Bayesian optimization loop. This system directly discovers ESP architectures that are Pareto-optimal for performance, efficiency, and robustness to input variations, ensuring designs are both high-performing and practically realizable.

ideas.title: ESPNet-Scale: Efficiency-Constrained Multi-Dimensional Scaling for High-Performance Lightweight Networks
ideas.description: This research proposes a principled framework for scaling efficient spatial pyramid networks (like ESPNet) without eroding their efficiency advantages. Instead of uniform depth/width scaling, we formulate scaling as a constrained optimization problem across multiple architectural dimensions—including depth, width, bottleneck ratios, and group sizes—under a target computational budget. The core innovation is an efficiency-aware neural architecture search or scaling policy that identifies the optimal combination of expansions to maximize accuracy for a given FLOP/parameter limit. The outcome is a family of models that systematically close the accuracy gap with larger networks while strictly preserving the lightweight, fast inference characteristics essential for edge deployment.

ideas.title: AutoESPNet: A Hardware-Aware Neural Architecture Search Framework for Adaptive Edge Deployment
ideas.description: This research develops AutoESPNet, a system that automatically optimizes the ESPNet architecture for any given edge device and deployment constraint. A lightweight, on-device profiler first characterizes the hardware's performance profile for key neural operations. This profile then guides a constrained neural architecture search over a pre-defined ESPNet super-network, identifying the optimal sub-network that meets specific latency, power, or memory budgets. The approach eliminates manual per-device engineering, enabling adaptive, Pareto-optimal model deployment across the diverse and evolving edge hardware ecosystem.

ideas.title: ESPNet as a Modular, Multi-Scale Feature Extractor via Hierarchical Signal Decomposition
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea of using ESPNet as a generic backbone by drawing an analogy from geophysical signal processing. In seismic data analysis, efficient extraction of multi-scale features is achieved by decomposing the complex input signal into parallel streams processed by specialized filter banks, followed by intelligent recombination. We adapt this solution by re-architecting the ESPNet backbone to explicitly decompose visual features into multiple parallel processing streams, each employing an ESP module variant tuned to a specific spatial or frequency scale. A lightweight, adaptive fusion mechanism would then synthesize these multi-scale representations. This creates a modular, 'ESP-based filter bank' backbone that can more efficiently capture the hierarchical structure crucial for diverse dense prediction tasks like semantic segmentation, depth estimation, and object detection, improving both performance and flexibility compared to a standard sequential ESPNet architecture.

ideas.title: Build a large ESPNet via Allometric Scaling Principles
ideas.description: Scale up ESPNet width and depth aggressively to compete with non-efficient models on server-side accuracy. Drawing an analogy from allometric scaling in biology, where organism traits scale non-linearly with size, we propose to solve this by not using uniform scaling. Instead, we will empirically derive architecture-specific scaling laws that govern the optimal relationship between dimensions like channel count, network depth, and receptive field size. The adapted idea is to build a large ESPNet by applying these discovered allometric scaling principles, co-scaling multiple dimensions according to power-law relationships to ensure the scaled model maximizes accuracy while preserving parameter and computational efficiency relative to its increased capability.

ideas.title: Accuracy-First Pruning via Thermodynamic Annealing Analogy
ideas.description: This idea inverts the typical priority of efficiency over accuracy by first guaranteeing SOTA accuracy, then compressing. The analogy comes from metallurgical annealing, where a material is first heated to achieve a target atomic structure (analogous to high accuracy) and then slowly cooled to optimize for strength and efficiency while preserving that structure. The adapted research idea is to design a two-phase training and compression framework. Phase 1 ('heating') trains a model to match or exceed SOTA accuracy without regard for size. Phase 2 ('slow cooling') applies a controlled, gradual compression process inspired by ESP principles, where pruning and quantization are constrained by a loss function that penalizes deviation from the Phase 1 accuracy, effectively preserving the high-accuracy 'state' while removing redundant parameters.

ideas.title: ESPNet with Metabolic-Flux-Inspired Adaptive Computation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from cellular metabolic regulation, this idea designs a dynamic-resolution ESPNet that adaptively adjusts computation based on input complexity and device load. In systems biology, cells use feedback mechanisms like the ATP/ADP ratio to sense energy state and substrate availability, dynamically regulating metabolic pathway flux for efficiency. The analogous solution is adapted by embedding lightweight, learnable 'allosteric sensor' modules within the ESPNet architecture. These sensors continuously monitor two streams: 1) input complexity metrics (e.g., local feature entropy, activation sparsity) and 2) real-time device resource metrics (e.g., available memory, thermal headroom). Their output jointly modulates the 'computational flux' by dynamically pruning or merging parallel convolutional branches in each ESP module and scaling the resolution of filters within active branches. This creates a network where computational graph and resolution are input- and context-dependent, mimicking a metabolic network that efficiently allocates enzymes and energy only where and when needed.

ideas.title: ESP-Gate: Lightweight Dynamic Fusion for Efficient Spatial Pyramid Networks
ideas.description: This work introduces a conditional gating mechanism to enhance the fusion stage of the Efficient Spatial Pyramid (ESP) module while strictly preserving its efficient decomposed convolution structure. Instead of a standard sum or concatenation, a tiny, computationally negligible gating network—composed of a few pointwise convolutions and a softmax—generates branch-specific blending coefficients conditioned on the input feature map. This allows the network to learn context-dependent, non-linear fusion strategies, improving representational power and task performance. The design maintains the original module's low parameter count and favorable hardware characteristics, making it a drop-in replacement suitable for real-time embedded vision applications.

ideas.title: Temporal Micro-ESPNet for Energy-Harvesting Video Scene Understanding
ideas.description: This research fuses the architectural goals of a microcontroller-optimized ESPNet with the task of video segmentation to create a novel 'Temporal Micro-ESPNet'. The core innovation is a lightweight, event-driven temporal fusion module integrated into the ultra-efficient micro-ESPNet backbone, designed to operate within the extreme power and memory budgets of energy-harvesting systems. This allows the model to maintain a minimal temporal context (e.g., via distilled frame differencing or micro-attention across sparse frames) for consistent video segmentation, which neither a static micro-model nor a standard video model alone could achieve. The outcome is a system capable of persistent environmental perception for applications like wearable assistive tech or smart sensors, where energy is sporadic and computational resources are severely limited.

ideas.title: Apply Multi-Objective Bayesian NAS with Evolutionary Primitives to ESP Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry and drug discovery, where multi-objective Bayesian optimization guides evolutionary search through a space of molecular primitives to design novel compounds. The analogous problem in ESP design is the search for optimal neural network module configurations under constraints like accuracy, latency, and power. The adapted solution defines a search space of ESP architectural primitives and employs a Bayesian Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework with an evolutionary algorithm. A surrogate model continuously learns to predict the performance of unseen configurations, guiding the evolutionary search to efficiently explore the Pareto frontier and automatically discover high-performing ESP architectures tailored to specific multi-objective hardware and task constraints.

ideas.title: Integrate Hierarchical Attention into ESP Module via Ecological Niche Partitioning
ideas.description: This idea proposes to combine spatial and channel attention with the Efficient Spatial Pyramid (ESP) module by drawing an analogy from ecological niche partitioning. In ecosystems, species specialize to exploit different spatial layers or resource types, increasing the community's overall efficiency. Adapting this solution, the research will design a hierarchical attention mechanism where parallel, specialized attention 'niches' focus on distinct spatial scales and channel groupings within the ESP's structure. A final gating network will dynamically integrate these specialized pathways, enabling the module to achieve improved feature discrimination and representation efficiency by capturing complementary information across scales and channels, much like a diverse ecological community optimally utilizes its environment.

ideas.title: Apply Ecological Succession Principles to Prune ESPNet
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from the field of Ecology to the problem of pruning ESPNet. In forest management, 'successional pruning' selectively removes trees based on their functional role, competitive pressure, and contribution to ecosystem health to guide development toward a stable, efficient state. The adapted research idea applies this principle by treating ESPNet's hierarchical convolutional modules as a developing ecosystem. A multi-stage pruning strategy is designed where components are not pruned based on simple magnitude thresholds, but on an analysis of their functional specialization, redundancy with adjacent modules (competitive pressure), and their role in the network's overall feature extraction stability. This biologically-inspired, guided approach aims to prune ESPNet into a more robust and parameter-efficient final architecture, mimicking how natural systems optimize structure for function.

ideas.title: Create a micro-ESPNet using biological metabolic pathway optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts metabolic pathway optimization from systems biology to create an ultra-efficient micro-ESPNet variant for energy-harvesting devices. In metabolic engineering, scientists design minimal, energy-efficient cellular pathways by eliminating redundant biochemical reactions while preserving essential functions. Similarly, we propose to design neural network architectures where each computational operation is treated like a metabolic reaction, systematically removing layers and connections that offer minimal accuracy gains for their energy cost. The resulting 'minimal inference pathway' network would operate within extreme power constraints of microcontrollers, maintaining core functionality through an architecture optimized for energy-accuracy efficiency rather than just parameter count reduction.

ideas.title: Event-Triggered Hierarchical Micro-ESPNet for Energy-Neutral Sensor Nodes
ideas.description: This research designs a micro-neural network architecture for energy-harvesting devices that fundamentally couples model execution with input saliency. The system employs a two-stage, always-on analog/digital event detector that activates a hierarchical, gated version of a micro-ESPNet only when necessary. This allows the network to operate in a near-zero power idle state, scaling its computational depth and width dynamically with the complexity of the detected event. The goal is to enable continuous ambient intelligence on power-frugal chips by making inference energy proportional to information utility, not just input presence.

ideas.title: Adaptive ESPNet via Ecological Niche Specialization Principles
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from evolutionary ecology, where generalist species diversify into specialists adapted to specific environmental niches, this research adapts ESPNet for diverse edge hardware. The analogous solution involves directed adaptation to exploit particular resource constraints. The adapted idea proposes an automated 'speciation' framework that evolves specialized ESPNet architecture variants from a common ancestor, each optimized through algorithmic mutation and selection for a distinct 'hardware niche'—such as mobile CPU cache profiles, DSP vector units, or microcontroller memory hierarchies—tailoring the network's operations, layer composition, and data flow to the precise constraints and strengths of each target edge device class.
